questions.stem: A 1-month-old infant presents with persistent jaundice and pale stools. The liver is enlarged and firm. The most likely diagnosis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Resolves by day 10-14 in term infants. NEVER causes pale/acholic stools or firm hepatomegaly. Pale stools = conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinaemia = red flag requiring urgent evaluation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Fibro-inflammatory obliteration of extrahepatic bile ducts. Classic: persistent jaundice + PALE/ACHOLIC STOOLS + dark urine + firm hepatomegaly at 2-6 weeks. Direct bilirubin elevated. Kasai portoenterostomy before day 60 = best outcome. Gold standard Dx: intraoperative cholangiogram.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Also causes neonatal jaundice + hepatomegaly, but KEY differentiator: vomiting + poor feeding + cataracts + reducing substances in urine after milk ingestion. Stools are NOT consistently pale.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Can cause conjugated jaundice + hepatomegaly but bile ducts are patent so stools are NOT consistently pale. Firm liver + pale stools + persistence is more specific for biliary atresia.

questions.keyPoint: PALE STOOLS beyond 2 weeks = BILIARY ATRESIA until proven otherwise. Refer urgently. Kasai must be done before day 60 for best prognosis.

rows: Acholic (pale) stools + dark urine in jaundiced infant
Cirrhosis; liver transplant if Kasai fails
Pale stools + jaundice beyond 2 weeks = biliary atresia

questions.stem: 6-YEAR-OLD BOY | RECURRENT EPISTAXIS | BRUISES | NORMAL PLATELETS + PT | PROLONGED aPTT A 6-year-old boy has recurrent epistaxis and multiple bruises. Platelet count and PT are normal; aPTT is prolonged. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Most common inherited bleeding disorder (autosomal dominant). VWF is a carrier for Factor VIII — deficiency causes MUCOSAL BLEEDING (epistaxis, bruising, gum bleeds) + prolonged aPTT (low FVIII activity). Diagnose with ristocetin cofactor assay.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Causes deficiency of factors II, VII, IX, X — prolongs BOTH PT and aPTT. Normal PT here excludes Vitamin K deficiency.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Factor VIII deficiency, X-linked recessive. ALSO gives prolonged aPTT + normal PT + normal platelets. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: Hemophilia A = HEMARTHROSIS + muscle hematomas. VWD = MUCOSAL bleeding (epistaxis, bruising). Epistaxis here points to VWD.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Liver disease prolongs BOTH PT AND aPTT (liver synthesises all clotting factors except VWF). Normal PT excludes significant hepatic disease.

questions.keyPoint: MUCOSAL BLEEDING + prolonged aPTT + normal PT + normal platelets = VWD. HEMARTHROSIS + same labs = Hemophilia A. This distinction is the most tested differentiator in paediatric bleeding disorders.

rows: VWD: autosomal dominant | Hemophilia A: X-linked recessive
Ristocetin cofactor assay (VWD) | Factor VIII level (Hemo A)
VWD: DDAVP + VWF concentrate | Hemo A: rFVIII

questions.stem: WEEKS AGO A 10-year-old girl has periorbital puffiness, cola-colored urine, and hypertension. She had a sore throat two weeks ago. Which test best confirms the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ASO titer is the most sensitive test confirming PRECEDING streptococcal pharyngitis. Rises within 1-3 weeks, remains elevated weeks-months. Gold standard CONFIRMING test for PSGN. Anti-DNAse B is even more sensitive for skin strep but not in options.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cultures active strep infection. At 2 weeks post-pharyngitis, strep is cleared — culture likely NEGATIVE. Tests current infection, not prior immunological trigger.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: C3 is LOW in PSGN (immune complexes activate complement). Supports the diagnosis and helps exclude lupus, but does NOT confirm streptococcal origin specifically. C3 normalises in 6-8 weeks.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ANA is positive in SLE. Would be used if lupus nephritis suspected. Normal C4 (vs low C4 in lupus) + preceding strep history points strongly to PSGN.

questions.keyPoint: PSGN confirmed by ELEVATED ASO TITER (evidence of recent strep pharyngitis). Classic: hematuria + hypertension + edema, 1-3 weeks post-pharyngitis. C3 low, C4 normal (alternative pathway).

rows: Cola urine + hypertension + edema 1-3 weeks post-strep pharyngitis

questions.stem: 4-YEAR-OLD GIRL | PETECHIAE AFTER VIRAL ILLNESS | PLATELETS 15,000/mm3 | NORMAL Hb | NORMAL WBC A 4-year-old girl develops petechiae after a viral illness. Platelets = 15,000/mm3; Hb and WBC are normal. Best management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: NOT routinely indicated in typical childhood ITP (post-viral, age 2-10, isolated thrombocytopenia, normal other cell lines). Reserved for atypical features or if starting steroids in some centres.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ITP is AUTOIMMUNE, not infectious. Antibiotics have no role. Antibiotics would be used if DIC from sepsis suspected, which would also affect Hb and WBC.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in acute childhood ITP. Reserved for CHRONIC ITP (>12 months) refractory to all medical therapy in children >5 years. 80% of acute childhood ITP resolves spontaneously within 6 months.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: First-line for platelets <20,000 or active bleeding. IVIG (1-2g/kg) = fastest response (24-72h). Prednisolone (2mg/kg/day) = also effective. For mild disease with only petechiae and no active bleeding, OBSERVATION alone is also acceptable in current guidelines.

questions.keyPoint: Childhood ITP: post-viral + isolated thrombocytopenia + normal Hb + normal WBC = ITP. Treatment for <20,000 or bleeding: IVIG (fastest) or steroids. NEVER splenectomy acutely. 80% self-resolve.

rows: IgG autoantibodies against GPIIb/IIIa on platelets

questions.stem: IMMUNOGLOBULIN LEVELS A 2-year-old boy with recurrent otitis media and chronic diarrhea is found to have absent tonsils and very low Ig levels. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Presents in 2nd-3rd DECADE. Tonsils PRESENT. Low but not absent Ig. Both sexes. Too young for CVID.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BTK gene mutation -> arrest of B cell maturation -> NO B cells -> NO immunoglobulin. ABSENT TONSILS (no B cells for lymphoid follicles) = PATHOGNOMONIC. Onset 6-18 months when maternal IgG wanes. Recurrent bacterial infections. ALL Ig classes profoundly low. MALES ONLY.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Only IgA is low; IgG and IgM are NORMAL. Tonsils present. Most common PID. Cannot explain absent tonsils or profoundly low all Ig.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Physiological in infants — maternal IgG wanes, infant production slow. Resolves by 18-24 months. Levels LOW but NOT absent. Tonsils present. No absent B cells.

questions.keyPoint: ABSENT TONSILS + very low ALL Ig + MALE + recurrent bacterial infections from ~6 months = XLA (Bruton's). Confirm: absent circulating B cells (CD19+). Treatment: monthly IVIG lifelong.

rows: Absent tonsils + absent lymphoid tissue + undetectable all Ig

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl with recent sore throat develops choreiform movements and emotional lability. Which antibody best supports the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Associated with SLE and autoimmune conditions. SLE can cause CNS manifestations but with other features (rash, nephritis). Not specific for Sydenham's chorea.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Associated with antiphospholipid syndrome (thrombosis + recurrent miscarriage). No relation to chorea after strep pharyngitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hallmark of MYASTHENIA GRAVIS — ptosis, diplopia, fatigable weakness. Completely different syndrome.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Most sensitive marker of recent GAS infection specifically for Sydenham's chorea. Remains elevated longer than ASO. Sydenham's = major Jones criterion for ARF. Chorea appears weeks-months after strep pharyngitis.

questions.keyPoint: Sydenham's chorea = major Jones criterion for ARF. Best antibody = ANTI-DNAse B (more sensitive for chorea than ASO). Chorea occurs weeks to months after strep pharyngitis.

rows: Carditis, arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules

questions.stem: NORMAL C3 A 6-year-old with generalised edema and frothy urine. BP normal. Proteinuria 4+, hypoalbuminaemia, normal C3. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (age 1-8). Massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminaemia + edema + hypercholesterolaemia + NORMAL BP + NORMAL C3. LM: normal. EM: podocyte foot process effacement. >90% respond to steroids.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NEPHRITIC syndrome: hematuria + hypertension + oliguria + LOW C3. Proteinuria is mild, not 4+. Normal BP and normal C3 here exclude PSGN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Typically causes hematuria + LOW C3 (persistent). Rare in young children. Normal C3 argues against MPGN.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Occurs in older girls/women with SLE features (rash, ANA+, anti-dsDNA+) + LOW C3 + LOW C4. Normal C3 + normal BP + no systemic features exclude lupus.

questions.keyPoint: NEPHROTIC SYNDROME in child age 1-8: edema + 4+ proteinuria + hypoalbuminaemia + NORMAL BP + NO haematuria + NORMAL C3 = MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE. Treat empirically with prednisolone WITHOUT biopsy.

rows: Podocyte foot process effacement; LM = NORMAL

questions.stem: KARYOTYPE 46,XX A 15-year-old girl with primary amenorrhoea and normal breast development is found to have absent uterus. Karyotype 46,XX. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Low GnRH -> no puberty -> ABSENT breast development (no oestrogen). This patient has NORMAL breasts = normal ovarian function. HH would show absent/arrested puberty.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Karyotype 45,X0. Short stature + streak gonads + absent puberty + webbed neck. Karyotype 46,XX rules out Turner completely.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: AIS = 46,XY. Normal female external phenotype + absent uterus (AMH from testes). KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: AIS = 46,XY; absent pubic/axillary hair. This patient is 46,XX — excludes AIS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 46,XX female with congenital absence of uterus and upper vagina (failed Mullerian duct development). Ovaries present and functional (normal oestrogen = normal breast development). No uterus = no menstruation = primary amenorrhoea. Normal female secondary sexual characteristics.

questions.keyPoint: PRIMARY AMENORRHOEA + NORMAL BREASTS + ABSENT UTERUS + 46,XX = MRKH. Normal breasts = functional ovaries. Absent uterus = Mullerian duct failure. 46,XX distinguishes from AIS (46,XY).

rows: Vaginal dilation or neovaginoplasty; IVF with surrogacy for fertility
AIS = 46,XY; absent pubic hair; testes present

questions.stem: OLIVE-LIKE MASS A 2-month-old infant vomits after every feed. Dehydration and a palpable "olive-like" mass are found. Expected acid-base disturbance?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pyloric stenosis = loss of HCl (gastric acid) via vomiting -> METABOLIC ALKALOSIS (H+ loss) + HYPOchloraemia + HYPOkalaemia (renal K+ excretion to conserve H+). Classic: HYPOCHLORAEMIC, HYPOKALEMIC METABOLIC ALKALOSIS. Palpable olive = hypertrophied pylorus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Occurs with hyperventilation (anxiety, early sepsis). Vomiting causes METABOLIC, not respiratory, disturbance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metabolic acidosis with high anion gap
choices.explanation: High AG metabolic acidosis = organic acid accumulation (DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, toxins). Pyloric stenosis = LOSS of acid -> alkalosis, not acidosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ot characteristic of pyloric stenosis. Mixed picture occurs in combined vomiting + diarrhea. Pyloric stenosis gives consistent metabolic alkalosis.

questions.keyPoint: PYLORIC STENOSIS = hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Projectile NON-BILIOUS vomiting at 2-6 weeks. Palpable olive = hypertrophied pylorus = PATHOGNOMONIC. USS confirms; Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after correcting electrolytes.

rows: Olive-shaped mass in right upper quadrant
Correct electrolytes FIRST, then Ramstedt pyloromyotomy

questions.stem: DESMOPRESSIN A 7-year-old has polyuria and polydipsia. Serum glucose normal; urine osmolality low; symptoms improve with desmopressin. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: idneys do NOT respond to ADH. Low urine osmolality persists AFTER desmopressin. This patient RESPONDS o desmopressin = rules out nephrogenic DI completely.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: M causes polyuria via osmotic diuresis. SERUM GLUCOSE WOULD BE HIGH. Normal glucose here excludes M.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: xcessive water intake. Urine osmolality rises with water deprivation (kidneys can concentrate). Desmopressin as minimal further effect. No improvement with desmopressin as cause is excessive drinking, not ADH eficiency.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DH deficiency from hypothalamus/posterior pituitary -> dilute polyuria. RESPONDS TO DESMOPRESSIN = onfirmatory. Common paediatric causes: craniopharyngioma, head trauma, histiocytosis X, idiopathic.

questions.keyPoint: RESPONSE TO DESMOPRESSIN = CENTRAL DI. NO RESPONSE = NEPHROGENIC DI. Normal glucose rules out DM. Water deprivation test: urine osmolality RISES with desmopressin in central DI.

rows: Urine osmolality RISES (central) vs NO RISE (nephrogenic)

questions.stem: ON ECHO A 5-year-old with persistent fever, cracked lips, conjunctivitis, rash, and coronary artery dilation on echo. Most important next step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: STANDARD OF CARE: IVIG 2g/kg (single infusion) + HIGH-DOSE ASPIRIN (80-100mg/kg/day). IVIG within 10 days reduces coronary aneurysm risk from ~25% to <5%. After fever resolves: switch to LOW-DOSE aspirin (3-5mg/kg) as antiplatelet.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Second/third-line for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki. NOT first-line.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Kawasaki is an immune-mediated VASCULITIS, not bacterial infection. Antibiotics have NO role and would delay definitive treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Controversial; some evidence in IVIG-resistant disease. NOT first-line standard of care. Aspirin + IVIG is the proven initial treatment.

questions.keyPoint: KAWASAKI = IVIG 2g/kg + high-dose aspirin within 10 days. IVIG dramatically reduces coronary aneurysm risk. Diagnosis: fever >5 days + 4/5 features (conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, extremity changes, cervical LN).

questions.stem: Rh-NEGATIVE MOTHER | NEONATE: SEVERE ANAEMIA + JAUNDICE + HEPATOSPLENOMEGALY | QUESTION: PREVENTION FOR FUTURE PREGNANCIES A neonate born to an Rh-negative mother has severe anaemia, jaundice, and hepatosplenomegaly. Preventive measure for future pregnancies?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Exchange transfusion TREATS the current affected baby. The question asks about PREVENTION for FUTURE pregnancies.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Anti-D Ig within 72 hours of delivery destroys fetal Rh+ RBCs in maternal circulation BEFORE mother forms lasting anti-D antibodies. Prevents sensitisation -> prevents HDN in future pregnancies. Also give at 28 weeks antenatally and after any sensitising event.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Treats iron deficiency anaemia. No role in preventing Rh incompatibility.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Prevents neural tube defects (taken before conception/first trimester). No role in preventing Rh HDN.

questions.keyPoint: Prevention of Rh HDN = Anti-D IMMUNOGLOBULIN within 72 hours of delivery (+ 28 weeks antenatally). Must give after ANY sensitising event: miscarriage, amniocentesis, CVS, abdominal trauma.

rows: Anti-D Ig: 28 weeks + within 72h postpartum + after sensitising events
Destroys fetal Rh+ RBCs in maternal circulation before antibody formation

questions.stem: A 5-year-old with SCD presents with sudden pallor and LOW reticulocyte count. Most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Sudden massive RBC trapping in spleen -> acute splenomegaly + hypovolaemic shock. RETICULOCYTE COUNT IS HIGH (compensatory). Low reticulocytes rules this out.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Accelerated RBC destruction. RETICULOCYTE COUNT IS HIGH (BM compensating). Rising bilirubin + falling Hb + HIGH reticulocytes. Low reticulocytes rules this out.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Paradoxical post-transfusion haemolysis. Variable reticulocyte count. Not typical without recent transfusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aplastic crisis due to parvovirus B19
choices.explanation: Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid precursors via P antigen -> HALTS ERYTHROPOIESIS temporarily. In SCD (short RBC lifespan ~20 days), even brief production halt causes dramatic Hb fall. KEY: VERY LOW RETICULOCYTE COUNT = bone marrow not producing. Self-limiting 7-10 days.

questions.keyPoint: LOW RETICULOCYTE COUNT in SCD = APLASTIC CRISIS (parvovirus B19 stops BM production). HIGH reticulocytes = haemolytic or sequestration (BM overworking). Reticulocyte count is THE key differentiator.

rows: Parvovirus B19 -> erythropoiesis stops -> LOW Hb + LOW reticulocytes
RBCs trapped in spleen -> LOW Hb + HIGH reticulocytes + splenomegaly
Accelerated destruction -> LOW Hb + HIGH reticulocytes + HIGH bilirubin

questions.stem: BLOOD FILM A 10-year-old boy develops pallor, jaundice, and dark urine after taking a medication. Blood film shows bite cells. Which enzyme deficiency is responsible?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Chronic haemolytic anaemia from birth. Autosomal recessive. Blood film: spherocytes or echinocytes. NO episodic trigger-related haemolysis. NOT bite cells.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Extremely rare cause of haemolytic anaemia. Not characterised by bite cells. Almost never the exam answer.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: G6PD deficiency: X-linked recessive. G6PD -> NADPH -> protects against oxidative stress. Without G6PD: oxidant drugs/fava beans -> Heinz bodies (denatured Hb) -> macrophages remove them -> BITE CELLS. Key: test G6PD level AFTER crisis resolves (reticulocytes give false-normal result during crisis).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Rare cause of hereditary haemolytic anaemia. Not associated with oxidant drug-triggered haemolysis or bite cells. Chronic, not episodic.

questions.keyPoint: BITE CELLS + episodic haemolysis triggered by oxidant drugs/fava beans/infections = G6PD DEFICIENCY. X-linked recessive. Test G6PD level AFTER acute crisis (reticulocytes give false-normal results during crisis).

rows: Bite cells + Heinz bodies (during haemolytic episode)
Oxidant drugs: primaquine, dapsone, nitrofurantoin, sulfamethoxazole; fava beans; infections
G6PD level AFTER crisis, not during
Avoid triggers; supportive; transfusion if severe

questions.stem: REFLEXES A 4-month-old with poor head control, frog-leg posture, hypotonia, and absent DTR. Which investigation confirms the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: easures cortical electrical activity. Used for epilepsy. SMA is a LOWER MOTOR NEURON problem (anterior orn cells). EEG would be normal.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CS shows reduced motor responses in SMA (supportive). Not CONFIRMATORY. Cannot distinguish SMA from ther LMN diseases without genetic confirmation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Genetic testing for SMN1 gene deletion
choices.explanation: MA = autosomal recessive deletion of SMN1 gene (5q) -> anterior horn cell degeneration -> progressive LMN eakness. Frog-leg + absent DTR + hypotonia in infant = SMA Type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffmann). SMN1 deletion esting (MLPA/PCR) = GOLD STANDARD. Treatment: nusinersen (intrathecal) or onasemnogene (gene herapy).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: K is ELEVATED in muscular dystrophies (Duchenne: >10x normal). In SMA, CK is NORMAL or mildly raised problem is nerve, not muscle).

questions.keyPoint: SMA TYPE 1: onset <6 months, frog-leg posture, absent DTR, hypotonia, NORMAL intellect. Confirm with SMN1 gene deletion. CK normal (vs high in Duchenne). Absent DTR = LMN (vs UMN in CP).

rows: Genetic testing — SMN1 deletion by MLPA/PCR
Onset <6mo; never sit; respiratory failure; most severe
Normal or mildly raised (vs very high in Duchenne)

questions.stem: A 3-year-old with cough, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots, and descending maculopapular rash. Most likely complication?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SSPE = classic TESTED long-term complication of measles. Persistent mutant measles virus in brain. Onset 5-10 years after measles. Progressive intellectual decline -> seizures -> myoclonus -> coma -> death. Koplik spots = PATHOGNOMONIC of measles. SSPE is the most frequently tested complication in MCQs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Coxsackie causes HFMD, herpangina, myocarditis. Completely unrelated to measles.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Rare in measles. Myocarditis is more classically associated with Coxsackie B. Measles most common acute complications = pneumonia and otitis media.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Measles may cause OTITIS MEDIA (not externa). However, even otitis media is a distractor here. SSPE is the most clinically important and most tested complication.

questions.keyPoint: MEASLES complications: Most common (acute) = pneumonia + otitis media. Most feared/tested (long-term) = SSPE (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis). Koplik spots = PATHOGNOMONIC of measles (white spots on buccal mucosa).

rows: Koplik spots — white spots on buccal mucosa, BEFORE rash
Maculopapular, starts at head, descends downwards

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy has recurrent infections, eczema, and thrombocytopenia. X-linked disorder suspected. Which immunological defect is present?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Combined B and T cell dysfunction
choices.explanation: Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome (WAS) = WASp gene mutation (X-linked). Classic TRIAD: ECZEMA + THROMBOCYTOPENIA (SMALL platelets = microplatelets) + RECURRENT INFECTIONS. Both T and B cell function progressively impaired: low IgM, variable IgG, high IgA + IgE. Risk of autoimmunity and lymphoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pure T-cell deficiency = DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion): absent thymus, low T cells, hypocalcaemia, cardiac defects. No eczema or thrombocytopenia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LAD = delayed cord separation + no pus + high WBC. Chediak-Higashi = albinism + giant granules. Neither has eczema + thrombocytopenia triad.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pure B-cell deficiency = XLA: absent tonsils + very low all Ig. No eczema or thrombocytopenia.

questions.keyPoint: WAS TRIAD: ECZEMA + THROMBOCYTOPENIA (SMALL platelets!) + RECURRENT INFECTIONS. X-linked. Combined B+T cell dysfunction. Microplatelets (small platelets) are PATHOGNOMONIC. Curative treatment = HSCT.

rows: Low IgM, variable IgG, HIGH IgA + IgE

questions.topic: Vitamin D Deficiency Rickets vs Hypocalcaemia Differentials
questions.stem: A 9-month-old exclusively cow's-milk-fed baby develops seizures. Labs: low calcium, high phosphate, low vitamin D. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: eneralised proximal tubular dysfunction -> phosphate WASTING (LOW phosphate, not high) + glucosuria + minoaciduria + renal tubular acidosis. Pattern here is opposite.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rickets due to vitamin D deficiency
choices.explanation: ow Vit D -> impaired Ca absorption -> hypocalcaemia -> (early/acute) before PTH compensation fully occurs + igh phosphate load from cow's milk -> this atypical picture. Context: cow's milk exclusively + low Vit D + ypocalcaemic seizures. Classic treatment: vitamin D + calcium supplementation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ow PTH -> LOW Ca + HIGH PO4 + NORMAL Vit D. Can cause hypocalcaemic seizures. However, Vit D would OT be low in primary hypoparathyroidism. Causes: DiGeorge, post-thyroidectomy, autoimmune.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: nd-organ PTH resistance -> LOW Ca + HIGH PO4 + HIGH PTH. Features: Albright hereditary osteodystrophy. are in a 9-month-old on cow's milk.

questions.keyPoint: Nutritional rickets in cow's milk-fed infants: low Vit D + hypocalcaemia + seizures. Classic rickets labs: LOW Ca, LOW PO4, LOW Vit D, HIGH ALP, HIGH PTH. High phosphate in this context = cow's milk high PO4 load + early Vit D deficiency.

rows: LOW Ca, LOW PO4, LOW Vit D, HIGH ALP, HIGH PTH
Low Vit D + high PO4 load = hypocalcaemia risk
Vitamin D supplement for all breastfed infants from birth
Craniotabes, rachitic rosary, bow legs, Harrison's sulcus

questions.stem: A 13-year-old with fever, sore throat, and splenomegaly. Peripheral smear shows atypical lymphocytes. Which test confirms the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Used for bacterial infections. EBV mononucleosis is VIRAL — blood cultures will be negative. Not the confirming test.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Detects HETEROPHILE ANTIBODIES that agglutinate horse/sheep RBCs. Rapid bedside test. Positive in ~90% of adolescents with EBV IM. Less sensitive in young children. If Monospot negative but IM suspected: EBV-specific antibodies (VCA IgM).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Paul-Bunnell = laboratory version of the same heterophile antibody test. Monospot is the modernised rapid version. Both detect the same antibodies — Monospot is current preferred clinical term.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Option not captured. Likely EBV serology (VCA IgM) = gold standard but slower.

questions.keyPoint: INFECTIOUS MONONUCLEOSIS (EBV): atypical lymphocytes + Monospot positive. Triad: fever + exudative pharyngitis + posterior cervical LN + splenomegaly. NEVER give amoxicillin (causes widespread rash). Avoid contact sports (splenic rupture risk).

rows: Splenic rupture, hepatitis, airway obstruction, encephalitis

questions.stem: A 15-year-old with T1DM presents with deep, rapid breathing and fruity breath odor. Underlying biochemical derangement?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: T1DM = INSULIN DEFICIENCY (autoimmune beta cell destruction). No insulin excess. In DKA, gluconeogenesis and lipolysis are INCREASED due to insulin deficiency.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: DKA = HYPERGLYCAEMIA (typically >250 mg/dL). Hypoglycaemia + hyperinsulinaemia = insulinoma. Opposite of DKA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Insulin deficiency -> lipolysis (fat breakdown) -> free fatty acids -> liver -> beta-oxidation -> acetyl-CoA -> KETONE BODIES (acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetone). Acetone = FRUITY BREATH. Ketone bodies (strong acids) -> metabolic acidosis -> compensatory KUSSMAUL breathing (hyperventilation to blow off CO2).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respiratory acidosis due to CO2 retention
choices.explanation: DKA = METABOLIC ACIDOSIS compensated by RESPIRATORY ALKALOSIS (hyperventilation). CO2 retention would cause respiratory ACIDOSIS. Kussmaul = compensation, not primary respiratory problem.

questions.keyPoint: DKA mechanism: Insulin deficiency -> lipolysis -> FFAs -> KETONE BODIES -> metabolic acidosis -> Kussmaul breathing. Fruity breath = acetone. Deep rapid breathing = metabolic compensation.

rows: High glucose + low pH + low HCO3 + high ketones + high anion gap
IV fluids (NaCl) + continuous IV insulin + K+ replacement
Cerebral oedema: headache + altered consciousness hours after treatment

questions.stem: 4-YEAR-OLD BOY | IRRITABILITY | PALLOR | PICA | Hb 8 g/dL | MCV 65 fL | LOW SERUM FERRITIN A 4-year-old boy has irritability, pallor, and pica. Hb = 8 g/dL, MCV = 65 fL, ferritin low. Most appropriate dietary advice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduce dairy products and add red meat
choices.explanation: REDUCE DAIRY: cow's milk displaces iron-rich foods AND calcium in dairy competitively inhibits iron absorption. Children drinking >600mL milk/day risk IDA. ADD RED MEAT: haem iron is most bioavailable form (2-3x better absorbed than non-haem). Most effective combined dietary change.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Vitamin C ENHANCES non-haem iron absorption when taken WITH iron food. Useful ADJUNCT but not PRIMARY intervention. Reducing dairy + adding red meat has greater impact.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Soy contains non-haem iron but also PHYTATES which INHIBIT iron absorption. Not superior to a mixed diet with red meat.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Folic acid treats MEGALOBLASTIC anaemia (HIGH MCV). This patient has MICROCYTIC anaemia (MCV 65 = low). Wrong type of anaemia.

questions.keyPoint: IDA DIETARY: (1) REDUCE COW'S MILK to <500mL/day. (2) ADD RED MEAT (haem iron). (3) Vitamin C with non-haem iron meals enhances absorption. PICA is PATHOGNOMONIC of iron deficiency.

rows: Low ferritin, low serum iron, HIGH TIBC, low MCV
Reduce dairy; add red meat; Vit C with meals

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy has limp and hip pain. X-ray shows flattening and increased density of the femoral head. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Posterior-inferior displacement of femoral head epiphysis in OBESE ADOLESCENTS (10-16y). X-ray: ice cream sliding off cone; Klein's line does not intersect femoral head. No increased density or flattening.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ACUTE onset with fever + severe pain + TOXIC appearance + inability to bear weight + systemic illness. X-ray: soft tissue swelling, no bony changes early. Requires URGENT joint aspiration + IV antibiotics.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Avascular necrosis of femoral head in children (4-10 years, boys>girls). Interrupted blood supply -> ischaemia -> bone death -> FLATTENING + INCREASED DENSITY (sclerosis) on X-ray. Limp + hip/knee pain, afebrile. MRI = most sensitive early investigation. Treatment: containment (bracing or surgery).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Most common cause of hip pain in children. Afebrile (or low-grade fever). NORMAL X-ray or mild effusion on USS. Normal inflammatory markers. SELF-LIMITING. Resolves with rest + NSAIDs.

questions.keyPoint: LCP = avascular necrosis of femoral head in boys aged 4-10. X-ray: FLATTENING + SCLEROSIS (increased density). Limp without fever. MRI = most sensitive. Koehler's disease = same process in navicular bone.

rows: Avascular necrosis of femoral head epiphysis
Flattening + sclerosis (increased density) of femoral head

questions.stem: A 4-year-old has a barking cough and inspiratory stridor worsening at night. Most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CROUP (Laryngotracheobronchitis): parainfluenza virus -> subglottic oedema -> BARKING cough + INSPIRATORY stridor + worse at night (steeple sign on X-ray). For moderate-severe croup: Nebulised epinephrine (rapid temporary relief) + Dexamethasone (reduces oedema, prevents rebound). For mild croup: oral dexamethasone alone suffices.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Option not captured.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Option not captured.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Option not captured.

questions.keyPoint: CROUP: barking cough + stridor + steeple sign. Mild = oral dexamethasone. Moderate-severe = dexamethasone + nebulised epinephrine. Parainfluenza virus most common cause.

rows: Steeple sign (subglottic narrowing on AP neck X-ray)
Thumb sign + toxic + drooling = epiglottitis; NEVER examine throat

questions.stem: SMEAR: BLASTS A 9-year-old with bone pain, pallor, hepatosplenomegaly, and lymphadenopathy. Peripheral smear shows blasts. Investigation that confirms diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DH is a PROGNOSTIC marker of tumour burden and cell turnover. Elevated in leukaemia. NON-SPECIFIC elevated in many conditions). Does NOT confirm diagnosis or classify leukaemia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LL diagnosis requires BMA (or biopsy) showing >20% blasts. Provides: blast percentage, cell morphology, mmunophenotyping (B vs T cell), cytogenetics (t(12;21) = good prognosis; Philadelphia chromosome = poor rognosis). Gold standard confirmatory test.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: dentifies antibodies on RBC surface. Used for AIHA and haemolytic transfusion reactions. No role in ALL iagnosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ssesses erythropoietic activity. Low in ALL (BM infiltrated by blasts). Does NOT confirm ALL diagnosis — only one marrow confirms.

questions.keyPoint: ALL confirmation = BONE MARROW ASPIRATION showing >20% blasts. Most common childhood cancer. Peak age 2-5 years. t(12;21) = best prognosis. Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) = worst prognosis.

rows: Children age 2-5; most common childhood malignancy
t(12;21); age 1-9; low WBC; B-cell ALL; female
t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome; T-cell ALL; age <1 or >10

questions.stem: HAEMATURIA A 7-year-old with sudden-onset purpura on lower extremities, arthralgia, and hematuria. Normal platelets. Had URTI 2 weeks ago. Most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IV steroids reserved for SEVERE HSP complications: severe abdominal pain, significant nephritis, severe joint involvement. Uncomplicated HSP with mild haematuria and arthralgia does NOT need steroids. Steroids do NOT prevent nephritis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: No established role in uncomplicated HSP. HSP has NORMAL platelets (vs ITP where IVIG is first-line).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Avoided in children with viral-associated illnesses (Reye's syndrome). Could aggravate renal or GI involvement in HSP.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Option d not fully visible in source file.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: IgA Vasculitis (HSP) is SELF-LIMITING in majority of children. Management: hydration + NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for joint pain + rest. Monitor for nephritis (urinalysis + BP weekly). Spontaneous resolution in 4-6 weeks. Only intervene with steroids for severe abdominal or renal involvement.

questions.keyPoint: HSP CLASSIC TETRAD: palpable purpura + arthralgia + abdominal pain + nephritis. Affects LOWER LIMBS. NORMAL PLATELETS (distinguishes from ITP). SELF-LIMITING in children. Monitor for nephritis = most serious complication.

rows: IgA immune complex deposition in small vessels
Palpable purpura + arthralgia + abdominal pain + nephritis
Supportive; steroids only for severe abdominal/renal involvement

questions.stem: 8-YEAR-OLD GIRL | FELL FROM 25th TO <5th PERCENTILE (2 YEARS) | BONE AGE DELAYED 2 YEARS | SLOW GROWTH VELOCITY An 8-year-old girl has fallen from the 25th to below the 5th percentile over 2 years, with bone age delayed by 2 years and slow growth velocity. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CGD = delayed bone age + NORMAL growth velocity (child follows their curve, just delayed). FAMILY HISTORY of late development. Child with CGD does NOT cross percentile lines downward. This girl is crossing lines = pathological.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CROSSING DOWNWARD PERCENTILE LINES + delayed bone age + DECREASED growth velocity (in absence of hypothyroidism). Bone age delay proportional to height retardation. Normal proportions. Screen: IGF-1 + IGFBP-3. Confirm: GH stimulation test. Treat: recombinant GH injections.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Also causes growth failure + delayed bone age BUT with other features: weight gain, constipation, dry skin, bradycardia, delayed milestones. Check TFTs in all growth failure workup — but isolated growth failure without systemic features favours GHD.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Short stature from birth + primary amenorrhoea + webbed neck + cardiac anomalies. Karyotype 45,X0. Features not mentioned here.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Malabsorption -> growth failure + diarrhea + abdominal distension + failure to thrive after gluten introduction. Screen with anti-tTG IgA.

questions.keyPoint: CROSSING PERCENTILE LINES DOWNWARD + delayed bone age + DECREASED growth velocity = GH DEFICIENCY. Bone age delay proportional. Confirm with GH stimulation test.

questions.stem: 12-YEAR-OLD BOY | POLYURIA + POLYDIPSIA + WEIGHT LOSS | RANDOM GLUCOSE 450 mg/dL | KETONURIA | DKA A 12-year-old with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, glucose 450 mg/dL, and ketonuria. Initial insulin regimen for DKA?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DKA PROTOCOL: After fluid resuscitation (0.9% NaCl 10-20mL/kg), start CONTINUOUS IV regular insulin at 0.05-0.1 u/kg/hr. Suppresses ketogenesis. Reduce glucose by ~50-100mg/dL/hr. Add dextrose to IV fluids when glucose <250mg/dL. Continue insulin until pH>7.3 and anion gap normalised.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Basal-bolus regimen is MAINTENANCE for well-controlled T1DM. NOT appropriate for acute DKA management. File answer is incorrect by ISPAD/ADA guidelines.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Older mixed insulin regimen for stable T1DM maintenance. No role in DKA.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: eactive, inadequate for DKA. Does not reliably suppress ketogenesis. No longer recommended as sole egimen.

questions.keyPoint: DKA INSULIN: Continuous IV regular insulin (0.05-0.1 u/kg/hr). Add dextrose when glucose <250 — but CONTINUE insulin until ketosis resolved. Subcutaneous insulin only when DKA resolved + patient eating.

rows: Glucose <250 mg/dL — keep insulin running to clear ketones
K+ falls with insulin — replace aggressively
Cerebral oedema: headache + altered consciousness hours after treatment

questions.stem: PEELING FINGERS | DILATED CORONARY ARTERIES In a 4-year-old with classic Kawasaki features and dilated coronary arteries, what is the critical complication?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Kawasaki disease with large coronary aneurysms -> CORONARY ARTERY THROMBOSIS -> MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION. Leading cause of death in Kawasaki. Aneurysms from vasculitis of coronary vessels. Thrombus forms within dilated turbulent-flow aneurysm. Prevention: aspirin (antiplatelet) + warfarin/heparin for giant aneurysms (>8mm).

questions.keyPoint: Kawasaki most critical complication = CORONARY ANEURYSM -> THROMBOSIS -> MI. Echo at diagnosis, 2 weeks, 6-8 weeks. Long-term aspirin mandatory for any aneurysm.

rows: IVIG within 10 days + aspirin; long-term anticoagulation for giant aneurysms
MI from coronary thrombosis in young children

flag.note: Recalled question — the source marks its options/answer as incomplete. Study the concept and verify the key.

questions.stem: MURMUR A 2-week-old infant with jaundice, feeding difficulties, hypoglycaemia (glucose 30), hypocalcaemia (Ca 6.5), and heart murmur. Most likely underlying disease?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DiGeorge (22q11 deletion): HYPOCALCAEMIA (absent parathyroids) + CARDIAC DEFECTS (conotruncal: TOF, interrupted aortic arch) + T-cell immunodeficiency (absent thymus). Hypoglycaemia is NOT classic for DiGeorge. Fits hypocalcaemia + heart murmur better.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BWS: macrosomia + HYPOGLYCAEMIA (hyperinsulinism from islet hypertrophy) + macroglossia + omphalocele + ear pits/creases. Neonatal hypoglycaemia = most dangerous feature. Heart murmur can occur. Jaundice + feeding difficulties from metabolic issues.

questions.keyPoint: BWS: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCAEMIA (most dangerous) from hyperinsulinism + macrosomia + macroglossia + omphalocele. DiGeorge: HYPOCALCAEMIA + cardiac + T-cell deficiency.

rows: FISH or chromosomal microarray for 22q11 deletion

questions.stem: PHARYNGITIS | LOW C3 | NORMAL C4 A 3-year-old with pre-orbital edema, hypertension, tea-colored urine 2 weeks after GAS pharyngitis. C3 low, C4 normal. True statement about this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resolves spontaneously in over 95% of cases
choices.explanation: PSGN in CHILDREN has EXCELLENT prognosis — >95% spontaneous resolution. Hypertension, haematuria, and oedema resolve in 2-4 weeks. C3 normalises in 6-8 weeks. Supportive care only for uncomplicated cases.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Immunosuppressive therapy NOT used in typical PSGN. Reserved for rapidly progressive GN (crescentic GN) = different, more severe entity.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: True but less specific and less high-yield than the spontaneous resolution statement.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PSGN = NEPHRITIC syndrome (haematuria + hypertension + oliguria). NOT nephrotic (massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminaemia). These are distinct syndromes.

questions.keyPoint: PSGN in children: >95% spontaneous resolution. LOW C3 + NORMAL C4 = alternative complement pathway = PSGN. C3 normalises in 6-8 weeks. No specific treatment for uncomplicated disease.

rows: LOW C3, NORMAL C4 (alternative pathway activation)

questions.stem: An 18-month-old with acute onset barking cough, stridor, and mild fever. Management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ROUP is VIRAL (parainfluenza). Antibiotics have NO role. Bacterial tracheitis/epiglottitis would need antibiotics different diagnosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ILD CROUP: single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15-0.6mg/kg. As effective as IM/IV. Benefits in 1-2 hours. educes duration and re-presentation. Discharge if improving.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: eserved for SEVERE croup or those unable to take oral medication. Oral is preferred for mild disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: or MODERATE-SEVERE croup or impending airway obstruction. Provides rapid but temporary relief (2-4 hrs, ebound risk). Not for mild uncomplicated croup.

questions.keyPoint: MILD CROUP = ORAL DEXAMETHASONE single dose. Moderate-severe = nebulised epinephrine + dexamethasone. Parainfluenza virus most common cause. Humidified air NOT evidence-based.

questions.topic: SLE — Target Antigen of Pathogenic Antibody
questions.stem: 12-YEAR-OLD GIRL | BUTTERFLY FACIAL RASH | ANA+ | ANTI-dsDNA+ A 12-year-old girl with butterfly facial rash, positive ANA, and positive anti-dsDNA. Target antigen for the pathogenic autoantibodies?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Anti-centromere antibodies = LIMITED SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (CREST syndrome). Not SLE.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Anti-Smith = most SPECIFIC for SLE (>99% specificity, ~25% sensitivity). However, the question specifically mentions anti-dsDNA positive antibodies — so the target antigen is dsDNA, not Sm.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Anti-dsDNA = most PATHOGENIC antibody in SLE. Target = double-stranded DNA. Titres CORRELATE WITH DISEASE ACTIVITY and nephritis flares. Forms immune complexes -> deposits in glomeruli -> lupus nephritis. The question specifically identifies anti-dsDNA antibodies — target = dsDNA.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anti-histone = DRUG-INDUCED LUPUS (hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid). No nephritis or CNS in drug-induced lupus.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Anti-topoisomerase I = DIFFUSE SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS + pulmonary fibrosis. No relationship to SLE.

questions.keyPoint: Anti-dsDNA = most PATHOGENIC in SLE (nephritis + titres track activity). Anti-Smith = most SPECIFIC for SLE (99% specific). Anti-histone = drug-induced lupus.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old with headache, vomiting, and ataxia. MRI shows large posterior fossa mass. Most common malignant brain tumour in this age and location?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Suprasellar location (NOT posterior fossa). Derived from Rathke's pouch. Presents with: bitemporal hemianopia + pituitary dysfunction + calcification.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Most common malignant brain tumour in ADULTS. Rare in children. Primarily in cerebral hemispheres. Not posterior fossa.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Most common MALIGNANT brain tumour in children. Location: POSTERIOR FOSSA (cerebellum). Features: headache + vomiting + GAIT ATAXIA. Arises from granule cell precursors (PNET). MRI: posterior fossa midline tumour. Can seed through CSF (drop metastases). Treatment: surgery + craniospinal XRT + chemotherapy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Also in 4th ventricle (posterior fossa) but LESS COMMON than medulloblastoma. Not the most common malignant type.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Most common BENIGN brain tumour in children (posterior fossa). WHO grade I. Excellent prognosis with surgery alone. NOT the most common MALIGNANT.

questions.keyPoint: Most common MALIGNANT brain tumour in children = MEDULLOBLASTOMA (posterior fossa). Most common BENIGN = pilocytic astrocytoma. Medulloblastoma: CSF seeding (drop metastases), craniospinal radiation.

rows: Medulloblastoma — posterior fossa, PNET, CSF seeding
Pilocytic astrocytoma — posterior fossa, WHO grade I

questions.stem: A 10-year-old with SCD has fever 39.5C and appears ill. Most critical initial action?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Antipyretics alone are INSUFFICIENT and dangerous. Febrile SCD = life-threatening infection risk. NSAIDs also carry renal risk in dehydrated SCD patients.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obtain blood cultures and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
choices.explanation: SCD = FUNCTIONAL ASPLENIA -> profound susceptibility to ENCAPSULATED ORGANISMS (pneumococcus, H. influenzae, meningococcus). Bacteraemia can rapidly progress to death. PROTOCOL: fever >38.5C in SCD = MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Blood cultures IMMEDIATELY then IV ceftriaxone WITHOUT WAITING for results.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LP only if meningitis clinically suspected (neck stiffness, photophobia, altered GCS). Fever alone does not mandate LP. Blood cultures + antibiotics FIRST.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Transfusion for specific crises: severe anaemia, acute chest syndrome. Fever alone is NOT a transfusion indication.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CXR important to rule out acute chest syndrome — but should NOT precede or delay blood cultures + antibiotics. Antibiotics first, then imaging.

questions.keyPoint: FEVER in SCD = MEDICAL EMERGENCY. PROTOCOL: Blood culture -> IV ceftriaxone IMMEDIATELY. Functional asplenia = cannot fight encapsulated bacteria. DO NOT DELAY antibiotics for results.

rows: Blood cultures -> IV ceftriaxone -> CXR + FBC + reticulocytes
S. pneumoniae + H. influenzae + N. meningitidis

questions.stem: TABLETS ON X-RAY A 4-year-old ingests ferrous sulfate tablets. Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain. X-ray shows radiopaque tablets. Definitive management for significant toxicity?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: WBI (PEG solution) = DECONTAMINATION tool. Removes unabsorbed tablets from GI tract. NOT definitive treatment of systemic toxicity.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CHARCOAL DOES NOT ADSORB IRON (LIMES: Lithium, Iron, Mercury, Ethanol, Strong acids = charcoal ineffective). Never use for iron poisoning.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: NAC = antidote for PARACETAMOL overdose. No role in iron poisoning.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Deferoxamine = IRON CHELATING AGENT. Binds free iron -> ferrioxamine complex -> excreted in urine (classic "vin rose" pink-orange urine). Indicated for: serum iron >500 mcg/dL or symptomatic ingestion (shock, altered consciousness, severe GI). Given IV continuously.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Rarely used now (iron tablets large, may not pass NG tube). WBI preferred for decontamination.

questions.keyPoint: IRON POISONING antidote = DEFEROXAMINE (IV chelation). "Vin rose" urine = treatment response. Charcoal is INEFFECTIVE for iron. WBI for GI decontamination only.

rows: Vin rose (pink) urine = ferrioxamine excretion = active chelation
Phase 1 GI -> Phase 2 latent -> Phase 3 systemic -> Phase 4 liver failure

questions.stem: REPLACEMENT >70% | NO ABNORMAL CELLS A 14-year-old with pancytopenia. Bone marrow: hypocellular with fat replacement (>70%), no abnormal cells. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALL = HYPERCELLULAR BM with >20% BLASTS. "No abnormal cells" rules out ALL completely.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MDS = dysplastic cells (abnormal morphology). Typically older patients. "No abnormal cells" rules out MDS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ANCYTOPENIA + HYPOCELLULAR BM with FAT REPLACEMENT + NO DYSPLASIA or blasts = APLASTIC NAEMIA. Diagnosis requires BM cellularity <25% (severe). Causes: idiopathic (autoimmune), drugs chloramphenicol, NSAIDs), radiation, viral hepatitis. Treatment: <40y + matched donor = allogeneic HSCT; thers = ATG + cyclosporine.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: arvovirus causes TRANSIENT aplasia of erythropoiesis only (NOT all cell lines) in haemolytic anaemias. Does OT cause pancytopenia with hypocellular BM in healthy children.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: DA causes microcytic hypochromic ANAEMIA only. NOT thrombocytopenia or leucopenia. BM shows erythroid yperplasia with absent iron stores — not hypocellular.

questions.keyPoint: APLASTIC ANAEMIA = HYPOCELLULAR BM with FAT REPLACEMENT + PANCYTOPENIA + NO DYSPLASIA or BLASTS. Must rule out leukaemia before diagnosis. Curative: allogeneic HSCT for young patients with matched donor.

rows: Hypocellular (<25% cellularity) with fatty replacement; no dysplasia

questions.stem: PULSES | LARGE UNREPAIRED PDA A 3-month-old with continuous "machinery" murmur at upper left sternal border and bounding pulses. Large PDA unrepaired. Most likely haemodynamic consequence?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: PDA = L->R shunt (acyanotic). Cyanosis only if EISENMENGER syndrome develops (late, irreversible reversal of shunt). Initial presentation is NEVER cyanosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Large PDA -> continuous L->R shunt -> pulmonary overflow -> INCREASED pulmonary blood flow -> PULMONARY HYPERTENSION. Eventually Eisenmenger syndrome: R->L shunt reversal -> differential cyanosis (cyanosed toes, pink fingers). Must close PDA before Eisenmenger develops.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Systemic hypertension = coarctation of the aorta. PDA causes increased PULMONARY pressure.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LVOTO = aortic stenosis or HCM. PDA = LV volume overload, not outflow obstruction.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PDA causes WIDE PULSE PRESSURE (bounding pulses from aortic diastolic run-off). Aortic systolic pressure maintained.

questions.keyPoint: Large PDA -> L->R shunt -> pulmonary overflow -> PULMONARY HYPERTENSION -> Eisenmenger. Close PDA BEFORE Eisenmenger develops (irreversible once R->L). Medical closure: indomethacin (premature neonates).

rows: Continuous machinery at left clavicle/upper LSB
Ductal-dependent lesions (pulmonary atresia — need PDA for pulmonary flow)

questions.stem: DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY A 2-year-old with progressive weakness, areflexia, and ataxia 3 weeks after diarrhoeal illness. NCS: demyelinating polyneuropathy. Treatment for potential complication?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: STEROIDS ARE CONTRAINDICATED IN GBS. Multiple trials show steroids do NOT improve and may WORSEN outcomes. Classic exam pearl — opposite of most inflammatory conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: GBS treatment: IVIG (2g/kg over 5 days) or PLASMA EXCHANGE — both equally effective. IVIG preferred in children. Work by removing/neutralising auto-antibodies against peripheral nerve antigens. Prevents/treats the complication of respiratory failure by shortening the course.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: GBS is AUTOIMMUNE triggered by molecular mimicry. The triggering infection has resolved. Antibiotics treat active infection — no role.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Treats herpes viruses. GBS is not treated with antivirals. Even in EBV-triggered GBS, IVIG is the treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Respiratory monitoring IS essential but supportive care ALONE is insufficient. IVIG or plasmapheresis are specific treatments that speed recovery and reduce respiratory failure risk.

questions.keyPoint: GBS = ascending flaccid paralysis + absent DTR + post-infectious. TREATMENT: IVIG or plasmapheresis. STEROIDS = CONTRAINDICATED. LP: albuminocytological dissociation (high protein, normal cells). Campylobacter = most common trigger.

rows: Albuminocytological dissociation: HIGH protein + NORMAL cells

questions.topic: Neonatal Jaundice — Physiologic vs Breast Milk
questions.stem: 7-DAY-OLD | BREASTFED | FEEDING WELL | NORMAL STOOL COLOUR | TOTAL BILIRUBIN 18 mg/dL | DIRECT 0.5 mg/dL (PREDOMINANTLY INDIRECT) A 7-day-old breastfed infant with jaundice. Feeding well, normal stools. Total bilirubin 18 mg/dL, direct 0.5 mg/dL. Most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Biliary atresia = CONJUGATED hyperbilirubinaemia. Direct fraction would be HIGH (>2 mg/dL or >20% of total). Here direct = 0.5 = predominantly INDIRECT. Also: PALE STOOLS (this patient has NORMAL stool colour). Both points exclude biliary atresia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Physiologic jaundice: INDIRECT hyperbilirubinaemia, feeding well, normal stools, peaks day 3-4, usually resolves by day 10-14 in term infants. Bilirubin 18 at day 7 is elevated but within the physiologic range (esp. in breastfed infants). Normal stool + normal feeding + indirect bili = physiologic pattern.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Peaks day 5-15, persists for weeks, indirect only, feeding well, normal stools. Cause: inhibitory substances in breast milk increase bilirubin enterohepatic circulation. Resolves if breastfeeding paused 24-48hrs. At day 7 this is the more accurate clinical diagnosis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Presents in FIRST 24 HOURS of life with rapidly rising jaundice. Not day 7. Positive Coombs test (not mentioned).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Absent/severely reduced UGT1A1 -> severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia from birth. Rare. Level of 18 possible in mild Type 2 but does not explain the feeding context better than physiologic/breast milk.

questions.keyPoint: INDIRECT hyperbilirubinaemia + feeding well + NORMAL stools in breastfed infant = physiologic or breast milk jaundice. DIRECT (conjugated) hyperbilirubinaemia is ALWAYS pathological regardless of age.

rows: Peaks day 3-4; resolves day 10-14; indirect only; term infant
Day 5-15; persists weeks; indirect only; pausing feeds diagnoses and treats
DIRECT bili elevated + PALE STOOLS + firm hepatomegaly
First 24 hours + positive Coombs + rapidly rising

questions.stem: An 11-year-old boy with tall stature, arm span > height, pectus excavatum, and arachnodactyly. Echocardiogram most likely reveals?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: oarctation = Turner syndrome + bicuspid aortic valve. The physical signs here (arachnodactyly, pectus xcavatum, arm span > height) = MARFAN SYNDROME, not Turner. This answer represents Turner-Marfan onfusion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ARFAN SYNDROME (FBN1, chromosome 15, autosomal dominant): Skeletal: tall + arachnodactyly + pectus eformities + arm span > height + scoliosis. Eyes: ectopia lentis (lens dislocation UPWARD). Cardiovascular: ORTIC ROOT DILATION (risk of Type A aortic dissection = major cause of death) + mitral valve prolapse. Echo s MANDATORY at diagnosis and annually.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: OF is associated with 22q11 deletion (DiGeorge), Down syndrome, or isolated. Not Marfan syndrome.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SD = Down syndrome, Noonan syndrome, idiopathic. Not a classic Marfan association.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: VP does occur in Marfan syndrome. However, the MOST IMPORTANT and most dangerous cardiac finding in arfan = AORTIC ROOT DILATION (risk of fatal aortic dissection). MVP is secondary.

questions.keyPoint: MARFAN SYNDROME: most important cardiac finding = AORTIC ROOT DILATION -> risk of Type A aortic dissection. MVP also occurs. Annual echo + beta-blockers to slow aortic dilation. FBN1 gene, chromosome 15, autosomal dominant.

rows: Tall + arachnodactyly + pectus + arm span > height + scoliosis
Annual echo; Beta-blockers to slow aortic dilation

questions.stem: HEPATOMEGALY | URINALYSIS: POSITIVE REDUCING SUBSTANCES A 6-month-old with listlessness, poor feeding, hypoglycaemia, lactic acidosis, and hepatomegaly. Urinalysis positive for reducing substances (not glucose). Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MSUD = deficiency of branched-chain keto acid dehydrogenase -> accumulation of leucine, isoleucine, valine. Classic: maple syrup odour of urine + neonatal encephalopathy. NOT positive reducing substances.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PKU = phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency -> accumulation of phenylalanine. Fair hair/skin + musty odour + intellectual disability if untreated. NOT positive reducing substances.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Classic galactosaemia (GALT deficiency): galactose accumulates -> jaundice + hepatomegaly + POSITIVE REDUCING SUBSTANCES IN URINE (galactose reduces Benedict's reagent but NOT Clinistix/glucose stick). Cataracts + hypoglycaemia + E. coli sepsis in neonates. Treatment: galactose-free diet. Gold standard: enzyme assay (GALT activity in RBCs).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: GSD type I (Von Gierke, G6Pase deficiency): hepatomegaly + hypoglycaemia + lactic acidosis + hypertriglyceridaemia. NO positive reducing substances in urine. Normal urine sugars.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: hypoglycaemia + fatty acid oxidation defect. Triggered by fasting. No positive reducing substances.

questions.keyPoint: GALACTOSAEMIA: positive REDUCING SUBSTANCES in urine (NOT glucose) + neonatal jaundice + hepatomegaly + cataracts + E. coli sepsis. KEY: Clinistix NEGATIVE but Clinitest (Benedict's) POSITIVE. Treat: galactose-free diet.

rows: Reducing substances in urine + cataracts + E. coli sepsis
GALT enzyme assay in red blood cells
Cataracts, cirrhosis, intellectual disability, ovarian failure

questions.stem: LETHARGY A 13-year-old girl with DKA, after starting fluid resuscitation and insulin drip, develops headache and lethargy. Most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Possible concern if glucose drops too rapidly. Symptoms: tremor, sweating, pallor, palpitations, confusion. However, the CLASSIC and most feared DKA treatment complication causing headache + altered consciousness = cerebral oedema. Monitor glucose and add dextrose if <250 mg/dL.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MOST FEARED COMPLICATION OF DKA TREATMENT IN CHILDREN (0.5-1% incidence, 20-25% mortality). Occurs 4-12 HOURS after starting treatment. Mechanism: rapid osmotic shifts with fluid/insulin therapy. Symptoms: HEADACHE + LETHARGY -> ALTERED CONSCIOUSNESS -> COMA -> HERNIATION. Treatment: IV MANNITOL + reduce fluid rate + neurosurgery if needed.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypernatraemia in DKA can occur with aggressive hypertonic saline but is not the classic complication causing headache + lethargy hours into treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Rare allergic reaction. Would present acutely with urticaria, anaphylaxis. Not headache + lethargy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Rare and unlikely in this context. SAH would be sudden-onset severe headache ("thunderclap"). Cerebral oedema is the overwhelmingly most likely and most tested DKA complication.

questions.keyPoint: CEREBRAL OEDEMA is the most FEARED complication of DKA treatment in children. Occurs 4-12 hours after starting treatment. HEADACHE + ALTERED CONSCIOUSNESS = emergency. Treat with IV MANNITOL immediately.

rows: Young age (<5), severe acidosis, high initial BUN, rapid fluid administration
IV mannitol 0.5-1g/kg + reduce fluid rate + head elevation

questions.stem: 4-YEAR-OLD BOY | PALLOR | Hb 5 g/dL | SPHEROCYTES ON BLOOD SMEAR | DIRECT COOMBS (DAT) NEGATIVE A 4-year-old boy with pallor and Hb 5 g/dL. Blood smear shows spherocytes. Direct Coombs test is NEGATIVE. Most likely inheritance pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Only 25% of HS cases are autosomal recessive. The MAJORITY (75%) are autosomal dominant. The question is asking for the "most likely" pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis is NOT X-linked. X-linked haemolytic anaemias include G6PD (bite cells) and CGPD variants. HS is autosomal.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hereditary Spherocytosis = mutations in RBC membrane proteins (spectrin, ankyrin, band 3, protein 4.2). MOST COMMON FORM (75%) = AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT. Spherocytes on smear + NEGATIVE DAT (distinguishes from AIHA which is also spherocytes + POSITIVE DAT). Gold standard diagnosis: EMA fluorescence cytometry (replaced osmotic fragility test).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Mitochondrial inheritance = passed through maternal line + affects organs with high energy demands. Not relevant to HS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: ~25% of HS cases are de novo mutations (no family history). However, "sporadic" is not the inheritance pattern of established HS cases. The most LIKELY inheritance pattern is autosomal dominant.

questions.keyPoint: HEREDITARY SPHEROCYTOSIS: Spherocytes + NEGATIVE DAT = HS (NOT AIHA). 75% AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT. Gold standard diagnosis = EMA fluorescence cytometry. Treat: splenectomy (age >6 years) + folic acid.

rows: 75% autosomal dominant; 25% AR or de novo
Folic acid + splenectomy (>6 years for cure)
Aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), pigment gallstones, haemolytic crisis

questions.stem: BIOPSY: THIN BASEMENT MEMBRANE ON EM A 10-year-old with haematuria, periorbital edema, and hypertension. Normal C3 and C4. Renal biopsy shows thin basement membrane disease on EM. Prognosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Poor, with progression to ESRD in adolescence
choices.explanation: ESRD progression is characteristic of ALPORT SYNDROME (COL4A3/A4/A5 mutation, X-linked or AR), which also shows thin BM on EM in early stages. However, Alport eventually shows progressive thickening + thinning + splitting of GBM + sensorineural hearing loss + ocular lens abnormalities.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Thin basement membrane nephropathy (TBMN / benign familial haematuria): mutation in COL4A3 or COL4A4 (heterozygous). Persistent microscopic haematuria, sometimes gross haematuria. NO proteinuria, NO renal failure, NO specific treatment. BENIGN lifelong course. Normal complement.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Corticosteroids are NOT used for TBMN. This is the standard teaching conflict flagged. Steroids are used for nephrotic syndrome (MCD), not for this benign haematuria condition.

questions.keyPoint: THIN BASEMENT MEMBRANE NEPHROPATHY = benign familial haematuria = EXCELLENT PROGNOSIS. No specific treatment. No steroids. Distinguish from ALPORT SYNDROME (progressive + hearing loss + ocular abnormalities).

rows: Thin Basement Membrane Nephropathy (Benign Familial Haematuria)
Excellent — benign course, no progression to ESRD
Alport = progressive ESRD + sensorineural hearing loss + X-linked

questions.stem: LONG-TERM CONTROLLER A 6-month-old boy with recurrent cough and wheezing in winter and positive family history of atopy. Most appropriate long-term controller?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Long-term oral steroids cause serious side effects (growth suppression, adrenal suppression, immunosuppression, osteoporosis). Reserved only for severe acute exacerbations or severe refractory asthma. Never first-line long-term controller.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: First-line CONTROLLER (preventive) medication for persistent asthma in children. Reduces airway inflammation, prevents exacerbations. Examples: fluticasone, budesonide, beclometasone. Use daily regardless of symptoms. SABA (salbutamol) used as RELIEVER for acute symptoms.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Leukotriene RECEPTOR ANTAGONISTS (montelukast) are step 2 add-on therapy, not first-line controllers. "Agonist" in the option is a mistake — there are no leukotriene agonists used clinically.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SABA (salbutamol) = RELIEVER medication for acute bronchospasm. Used PRN. NOT a long-term controller. Using SABA alone without ICS = inadequate control in persistent asthma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Antihistamines treat allergic rhinitis and urticaria. Do NOT treat airway inflammation or bronchoconstriction in asthma. No role as asthma controller.

questions.keyPoint: PERSISTENT ASTHMA long-term controller = INHALED CORTICOSTEROIDS (ICS). ICS = controller (daily, preventive). SABA = reliever (PRN, acute). Step up: ICS + LABA. Never use oral steroids long-term.

questions.stem: SUPINE | LATERAL NECK X-RAY: THUMB SIGN A 2-year-old with high fever, drooling, toxic appearance, refusal to swallow, and worsening SOB when supine. Lateral neck X-ray shows "thumb sign". Best initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endotracheal intubation in a controlled setting (theatre)
choices.explanation: Epiglottitis = LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY. Thumb sign on lateral X-ray = swollen epiglottis. PROTOCOL: NEVER examine throat (can cause complete obstruction). Call anaesthetist IMMEDIATELY. Take child to THEATRE (not emergency department). Secure airway (intubate) under controlled conditions. Antibiotics (ceftriaxone) after airway secured.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Nebulised epinephrine = for CROUP (subglottic narrowing). Examining/nebulising a child with epiglottitis can precipitate complete airway obstruction. Dangerous.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ABSOLUTELY DANGEROUS. Epiglottitis progresses rapidly to complete obstruction and death. Any delay in airway management is life-threatening.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Antibiotics ARE needed (H. influenzae type b, S. aureus) but ONLY AFTER airway is secured. Starting antibiotics without securing the airway first is inadequate and dangerous.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Corticosteroids are for CROUP, not epiglottitis. And oral medications can trigger coughing/vomiting = obstruction risk in epiglottitis.

questions.keyPoint: EPIGLOTTITIS: NEVER examine throat. CALL ANAESTHETIST. GO TO THEATRE. INTUBATE. Then antibiotics. The "3 NEVERs": Never examine throat, Never put in supine, Never leave child alone.

rows: H. influenzae type b (classic); now also S. aureus (post-Hib vaccine)
Thumb sign (swollen epiglottis on lateral neck X-ray)
Examine throat / leave alone / put supine
Theatre -> controlled intubation -> IV ceftriaxone after airway

questions.stem: PANSYSTOLIC MURMUR AT LOWER LEFT STERNAL BORDER | INCREASED PULMONARY MARKINGS ON CXR A 6-month-old with recurrent chest infections, sweating during feeding, failure to thrive, pansystolic murmur at lower left sternal border, and increased pulmonary markings. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ASD = ejection systolic murmur + FIXED SPLIT S2 + right heart dilatation. Pansystolic murmur at LLSB is NOT ASD.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PDA = CONTINUOUS MACHINERY murmur at upper left sternal border (left clavicle area) + bounding pulses. NOT pansystolic at LLSB.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: VSD = PANSYSTOLIC murmur best heard at LOWER LEFT STERNAL BORDER (4th intercostal space). L->R shunt -> pulmonary overflow -> increased pulmonary markings on CXR + recurrent chest infections + failure to thrive + sweating with feeding (increased sympathetic activity from cardiac work). Most common congenital heart disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TOF = CYANOTIC heart disease. Ejection systolic murmur at upper left sternal border (pulmonary stenosis). Boot-shaped heart on CXR. Cyanosis (not described here).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PS = ejection systolic murmur at upper left sternal border + normal or decreased pulmonary vascularity. Increased pulmonary markings argue against PS.

questions.keyPoint: VSD = pansystolic murmur at LOWER LEFT STERNAL BORDER. L->R shunt = increased pulmonary blood flow + failure to thrive + sweating. MOST COMMON congenital heart disease. Small VSDs may close spontaneously.

rows: Pansystolic at lower left sternal border
VSD is most common congenital heart defect

questions.stem: SPLIT S2 A 7-year-old with exertional dyspnoea, recurrent chest infections, soft systolic murmur, and FIXED SPLIT S2. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: VSD = PANSYSTOLIC murmur at LLSB. No fixed split S2. Different location and character.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PDA = CONTINUOUS machinery murmur at upper left clavicle. No fixed split S2.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ASD = FIXED SPLIT S2 (pathognomonic) + soft ejection systolic murmur at upper left sternal border (from increased pulmonary flow). L->R shunt -> right heart volume overload -> right atrial and right ventricular enlargement -> fixed split S2 (right bundle branch delay persists regardless of respiration). Often asymptomatic in childhood; presents with exertional dyspnoea + recurrent chest infections.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PS = ejection systolic murmur + variable S2 (wide split that VARIES with respiration — not fixed). Post-stenotic dilatation on CXR.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TOF = cyanotic + ejection systolic murmur (PS component) + right aortic arch on CXR. Cyanosis and clubbing expected. Fixed split S2 is not a TOF feature.

questions.keyPoint: FIXED SPLIT S2 = ASD (pathognomonic). Fixed = does not change with respiration. Soft ejection systolic murmur + right heart dilatation on echo. ASD most common to present in adulthood if missed in childhood.

rows: Fixed split S2 (does not vary with respiration)
Soft ejection systolic at upper LSB (pulmonary flow murmur)

questions.stem: A 3-day-old neonate presents with progressive jaundice and rapidly rising DIRECT bilirubin. Most likely underlying pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Physiological jaundice = INDIRECT (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinaemia. A rising DIRECT bilirubin is ALWAYS pathological and is NEVER physiological.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Breast milk jaundice = INDIRECT (unconjugated) only. Rising direct bilirubin is NOT caused by breast milk jaundice.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: RISING DIRECT BILIRUBIN in a neonate = biliary atresia until proven otherwise. Fibro-inflammatory obliteration of bile ducts -> conjugated bili unable to be excreted -> rises rapidly. Also: pale stools + dark urine + firm hepatomegaly. Urgent investigation needed: hepatobiliary HIDA scan -> intraoperative cholangiogram -> Kasai before day 60.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Breastfeeding jaundice (early, <72hrs): INDIRECT only, from insufficient milk intake and enterohepatic circulation. NOT direct hyperbilirubinaemia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: HDN (Rh or ABO incompatibility): INDIRECT (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinaemia from haemolysis. NOT direct. Rising direct bilirubin excludes HDN.

questions.keyPoint: RISING DIRECT BILIRUBIN in any neonate = ALWAYS PATHOLOGICAL. Biliary atresia is the most common serious cause. Other causes: neonatal hepatitis, choledochal cyst, Alagille syndrome, metabolic liver disease.

rows: Direct bilirubin = ALWAYS pathological; ALWAYS investigate
Most common serious cause of neonatal conjugated jaundice

questions.stem: NORMAL SERUM ALBUMIN A child with cola-colored urine, haematuria on dipstick, hypertension, and sore throat 2 weeks ago. Normal serum albumin. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Classic PSGN: NEPHRITIC syndrome (haematuria + hypertension + oliguria + oedema) + preceding strep pharyngitis 1-3 weeks ago + LOW C3 + NORMAL albumin (not nephrotic). Normal albumin differentiates from nephrotic syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: IgA nephropathy (Berger's disease): haematuria within 1-3 DAYS of infection (synpharyngitic, NOT 2 weeks later). Typically young adult males. Normal complement.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Nephrotic: massive proteinuria + LOW albumin + oedema + normal BP. This patient has NORMAL albumin + hypertension. Normal albumin excludes nephrotic syndrome.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HUS: MAHA (microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia) + thrombocytopenia + AKI. Typically after E. coli O157:H7 (bloody diarrhoea). No preceding pharyngitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SLE features: butterfly rash + ANA+ + anti-dsDNA+ + LOW C3 + LOW C4. No systemic lupus features described here. 2-week post-pharyngitis latency points firmly to PSGN.

questions.keyPoint: PSGN = NEPHRITIC syndrome (haematuria + hypertension + oliguria + oedema) 1-3 WEEKS after strep pharyngitis. NORMAL albumin (not nephrotic). IgA nephropathy = SYNPHARYNGITIC (concurrent, not 2 weeks later).

rows: IgA = concurrent haematuria during infection (<5 days)

questions.stem: A 5-year-old with fever and parotid swelling, incompletely vaccinated. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Bacterial (suppurative) parotitis = typically unilateral + exquisitely tender + pus from Stensen's duct + elderly/debilitated/dehydrated patients. Caused by S. aureus. Not described here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Mumps (Paramyxovirus): bilateral parotid swelling + fever + incomplete vaccination = MUMPS until proven otherwise. Other features: orchitis (males, post-pubertal), aseptic meningitis, pancreatitis. Prevention: MMR vaccine (2 doses).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dental abscess = unilateral + dental pain + localised swelling + associated with dental pathology. NOT bilateral parotid gland swelling.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Measles = fever + cough + conjunctivitis + Koplik spots + descending maculopapular rash. Does NOT cause parotid swelling.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Lymphadenitis = swollen lymph nodes (discrete, moveable). Parotid swelling is a GLAND (not lymph node), located in front of the ear/over the masseter. Different anatomy.

questions.keyPoint: MUMPS = bilateral parotid swelling + fever + incomplete MMR vaccination. Complications: orchitis (post-pubertal males, 30% = unilateral atrophy), aseptic meningitis, pancreatitis, sensorineural deafness.

questions.stem: CIRCULATION A newborn has severe central cyanosis that does NOT improve with supplemental O2. Echo shows parallel circulation. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GA = aorta arises from RIGHT ventricle; pulmonary artery from LEFT ventricle = PARALLEL CIRCULATION systemic and pulmonary circulations are SEPARATE, not in series). Severe central cyanosis from BIRTH. O2 oes NOT help (blood cannot reach the lungs for oxygenation in useful way). Survival depends on mixing (PFO, SD, VSD, or PDA). Emergency: prostaglandin E1 (keep ductus open) + Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy. efinitive: arterial switch operation (Jatene procedure).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: OF = cyanotic CHD (right-to-left shunt through VSD + pulmonary stenosis). However, cyanosis is VARIABLE tet spells), not fixed severe cyanosis from birth. TOF has RIGHT ventricular outflow obstruction, not parallel irculation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PHN: high pulmonary vascular resistance -> R->L shunt through PFO/PDA. Associated with hypoxic birth, econium aspiration. O2 MAY help by reducing pulmonary vasoconstriction. Not parallel circulation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: solated PS = right-sided obstruction. Causes cyanosis only if severe (critical PS with R->L shunt through PFO). ot parallel circulation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: otal anomalous pulmonary venous return: pulmonary veins drain to right heart. Cyanosis + pulmonary oedema. ot parallel circulation.

questions.keyPoint: TGA: parallel circulation = SEVERE cyanosis from birth + O2 DOES NOT HELP. Emergency Rx: Prostaglandin E1 (keep PDA open for mixing) + Rashkind septostomy. Definitive: arterial switch operation.

rows: Aorta from RV + PA from LV = parallel circulation, no effective oxygenation
Severe cyanosis at birth; O2 does not help; egg on string CXR
Prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) + Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy
Arterial switch operation (Jatene procedure) within first 2 weeks

questions.stem: A 5-day-old preterm neonate has bloody stools, abdominal distension, and pneumatosis intestinalis on X-ray. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meconium ileus: failure to pass meconium in first 48 hours + abdominal distension + small bowel obstruction. Associated with CYSTIC FIBROSIS. No pneumatosis intestinalis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hirschsprung: delayed meconium (>48hrs) + chronic constipation + abdominal distension + explosive stool with rectal exam. No bloody stools or pneumatosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sepsis: temperature instability + poor feeding + respiratory distress + elevated inflammatory markers. Can cause secondary bowel involvement but PNEUMATOSIS INTESTINALIS is specifically NEC, not simple sepsis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: NEC = PNEUMATOSIS INTESTINALIS (gas in bowel wall from bacterial invasion) = PATHOGNOMONIC. Classic: preterm neonate + bloody stools + abdominal distension + pneumatosis on X-ray (or portal venous gas = severe). Treatment: NPO + IV antibiotics + surgical resection for perforation/necrosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Atresia: BILIOUS vomiting + failure to pass meconium + X-ray shows few air-fluid levels + double-bubble (duodenal). No pneumatosis intestinalis.

questions.keyPoint: PNEUMATOSIS INTESTINALIS = NEC until proven otherwise. Preterm + bloody stools + abdominal distension + pneumatosis = NEC. Prevention: breastfeeding reduces NEC risk by 6-10x vs formula.

rows: Portal venous gas = indicates systemic involvement
NPO + NG decompression + IV antibiotics + surgical resection for necrosis/perforation

questions.stem: An infant with headache and neck stiffness. CSF: neutrophilia, low glucose, high protein. Next best step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Oral antibiotics have inadequate CNS penetration for bacterial meningitis. IV route is mandatory for adequate CSF levels.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bacterial meningitis (neutrophilic pleocytosis + low glucose + high protein) = MEDICAL EMERGENCY. START IV ANTIBIOTICS IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT wait for CSF culture results. Typical regimen: IV ceftriaxone + IV dexamethasone (to reduce inflammation). In neonates: ampicillin + gentamicin + cefotaxime (covers GBS, Listeria, Gram-negatives).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dexamethasone ALONE is INSUFFICIENT and dangerous. Must be given WITH antibiotics. Dexamethasone reduces meningeal inflammation and hearing loss risk (especially for H. influenzae meningitis).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: IM route is used if IV access cannot be obtained rapidly. IV is preferred for reliable pharmacokinetics in meningitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Observation = FATAL. Bacterial meningitis has high mortality without immediate treatment. Every hour of delay increases mortality and neurological damage.

questions.keyPoint: Bacterial meningitis: NEUTROPHILIC CSF + low glucose + high protein = IV ANTIBIOTICS IMMEDIATELY + dexamethasone. Do NOT wait for culture results. Every hour of delay = worse outcome.

rows: Turbid; neutrophilia; LOW glucose (<40% serum); HIGH protein
Reduces hearing loss (especially in H. influenzae type b)
Hearing loss, cerebral abscess, hydrocephalus, death

questions.stem: An infant with cough, wheezing, feeding difficulty, and fever. RSV positive. Best initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supportive care with hydration and O2 if needed
