choices.explanation: RSV BRONCHIOLITIS management = SUPPORTIVE ONLY. (1) O2 for SpO2 <92% (2) IV/NG tube feeds if unable to feed (3) Nasal suctioning + humidified air. NO antibiotics (viral), NO bronchodilators (not evidence-based in RCTs), NO steroids (no benefit). Ribavirin only for severe immunocompromised cases.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bronchiolitis is VIRAL (RSV, rhinovirus, metapneumovirus). Antibiotics have NO role unless secondary bacterial infection suspected.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bronchodilators (salbutamol) are NOT evidence-based in bronchiolitis. RCTs show NO benefit. Not recommended in current guidelines (NICE, AAP).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Steroids NOT recommended for bronchiolitis. Unlike asthma (reversible bronchoconstriction), bronchiolitis = mucosal inflammation + mucus plugging. Steroids do not help.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No role in bronchiolitis. Antihistamines treat allergic conditions. Not indicated here.

questions.keyPoint: RSV BRONCHIOLITIS = SUPPORTIVE CARE ONLY. O2 + hydration + suctioning. No antibiotics, No bronchodilators, No steroids. This is a classic exam question about what NOT to do in bronchiolitis.

rows: SpO2 <92%, inability to feed, severe respiratory distress

questions.stem: A 12-month-old infant sits well and babbles, but cannot stand unsupported. Which developmental domain is delayed?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GROSS MOTOR milestones at 12 months: STANDS UNSUPPORTED + cruises furniture + takes first steps (or 1-2 steps). Cannot stand unsupported at 12 months = GROSS MOTOR DELAY. Sitting (achieved by 6 months) is appropriate.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ne motor at 12 months: pincer grasp (thumb + index finger) + points + bangs objects together. Sitting uses oss motor. Fine motor skills are not described as delayed.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: nguage at 12 months: says "mama/dada" meaningfully + 1-3 words + babbles. The infant is babbling = nguage appropriate for stage.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: cial at 12 months: stranger anxiety + separation anxiety + waves bye-bye + plays peek-a-boo. Not described delayed.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: gnitive development (object permanence by 8-9 months) not specifically described as delayed. The finding is ecifically gross motor.

questions.keyPoint: GROSS MOTOR MILESTONES: Head control 3-4m, Sits 6m, Crawls 9m, STANDS UNSUPPORTED 12m, Walks 12-15m, Runs 18m. Cannot stand at 12 months = GROSS MOTOR DELAY. Rule out: SMA, cerebral palsy, Duchenne MD.

rows: Not sitting by 9m; not standing by 14m; not walking by 18m

questions.stem: RETICULONODULAR PATTERN + AIR BRONCHOGRAMS A 30-week preterm neonate has respiratory distress, nasal flaring, and subcostal retractions. CXR: reticulonodular pattern with air bronchograms. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TTN: term or near-term infant, born by C-section. Retained lung fluid. CXR: streaky perihilar markings + fluid in fissures. NOT reticulonodular pattern. Resolves in 24-72 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MAS: post-term or stressed neonate + green amniotic fluid + meconium on vocal cords. CXR: hyperinflation + patchy asymmetric infiltrates. NOT reticulonodular pattern.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Preterm + surfactant deficiency -> alveolar collapse -> RETICULONODULAR (ground-glass) PATTERN + AIR BRONCHOGRAMS (open bronchi silhouetted against collapsed alveoli) on CXR. Treatment: exogenous surfactant (endotracheal) + CPAP or mechanical ventilation. Prevention: antenatal steroids (betamethasone) to mother.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pneumonia: consolidation on CXR (not ground-glass/reticulonodular). Inflammatory infiltrates. Associated with risk factors (PROM, maternal fever, GBS).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PPHN: high pulmonary resistance -> R->L shunt -> cyanosis + CXR showing oligaemic lung fields (normal or dark). No reticulonodular pattern.

questions.keyPoint: NEONATAL RDS: PRETERM + surfactant deficiency + RETICULONODULAR PATTERN (ground glass) + AIR BRONCHOGRAMS on CXR. Treatment: exogenous surfactant. Prevention: antenatal steroids.

rows: Antenatal steroids (betamethasone IM) to mother if preterm delivery expected

questions.stem: A 6-year-old with developmental regression and macrocephaly. MRI shows demyelination of white matter. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Rett syndrome: FEMALES ONLY (MECP2 mutation, X-linked). Normal development until 6-18 months then REGRESSION + hand-wringing stereotypies + autistic features. Not typically associated with white matter demyelination on MRI.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MLD (Arylsulfatase A deficiency, AR): lysosomal storage disorder -> accumulation of sulphatides in CNS WHITE MATTER + peripheral nerves -> DEMYELINATION + REGRESSION. MRI: BILATERAL SYMMETRICAL white matter signal on T2. Late infantile type: regression of motor skills + gait atrophy. Macrocephaly from white matter expansion. Definitive: enzyme assay (arylsulfatase A activity).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CP is NON-PROGRESSIVE (static brain injury). Developmental regression implies PROGRESSIVE disease. CP does not progress and typically presents with tone abnormalities, not regression.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tay-Sachs (Hex A deficiency): cherry-red spot on fundoscopy + progressive neurodegeneration + GREY MATTER predominantly (not primarily white matter). Onset earlier (3-6 months). Predominantly affects Jewish population.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: ALD (X-linked, ABCD1 gene): demyelination predominantly in POSTERIOR WHITE MATTER. Affects MALES. Associated with ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (skin hyperpigmentation). Usually later onset (5-10 years).

questions.keyPoint: MLD: white matter demyelination + developmental regression + BILATERAL SYMMETRICAL white matter on MRI + macrocephaly. Arylsulfatase A deficiency. Enzyme assay = gold standard diagnosis.

rows: Bilateral symmetrical white matter T2 signal (demyelination)
Enzyme assay: arylsulfatase A activity in leucocytes

questions.stem: A child with fever, strawberry tongue, cracked lips, elevated ESR and CRP. Best initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Aspirin alone is INSUFFICIENT. While high-dose aspirin is part of Kawasaki treatment, IVIG is the key intervention that reduces coronary aneurysm risk. Without IVIG, aneurysm risk remains ~25%.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: IVIG (2g/kg IV) + high-dose aspirin together = STANDARD OF CARE. The question asks for BEST INITIAL management — IVIG is the critical component. IVIG within 10 days of fever onset reduces coronary aneurysm risk from ~25% to <5%.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Kawasaki is VASCULITIS, not bacterial infection. Antibiotics have NO role.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Corticosteroids controversial; may be used in IVIG-resistant cases. NOT first-line alone.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Observation without treatment = high risk of coronary aneurysms (potentially fatal MI in a child). Unacceptable.

questions.keyPoint: KAWASAKI BEST INITIAL Rx = IVIG 2g/kg + high-dose aspirin. IVIG is the critical component that prevents coronary aneurysms. Must be given within 10 days of fever onset.

rows: High-dose (80-100mg/kg/day) acute phase -> low-dose after defervescence
Repeat IVIG dose or corticosteroids or infliximab

questions.stem: A 12-year-old with migratory polyarthritis, recent sore throat, cough, and new murmur. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ARF = Jones criteria: MAJOR: carditis (new murmur) + MIGRATORY POLYARTHRITIS + chorea + erythema marginatum + subcutaneous nodules. MINOR: fever + elevated ESR/CRP + prolonged PR interval + previous ARF. Two major OR one major + two minor + evidence of preceding strep infection (elevated ASO/anti-DNAse B) = diagnosis. Migratory arthritis + carditis (new murmur) + post-streptococcal = ARF.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: IE: fever + new murmur + risk factors (structural heart disease, IV drug use) + embolic phenomena (Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, Roth spots). No migratory arthritis. Positive blood cultures.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: JIA: PERSISTENT arthritis >6 weeks in children <16 years. NOT migratory. Different pattern of joint involvement. No preceding strep infection or carditis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Myocarditis: chest pain + dyspnoea + arrhythmia + elevated troponin. May have murmur from valve dysfunction. No migratory arthritis or strep connection.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SLE: butterfly rash + ANA+ + anti-dsDNA+ + multi-organ involvement. Arthritis in SLE is typically non-migratory, non-erosive. No strep connection.

questions.keyPoint: ARF JONES CRITERIA: Major = Carditis, Arthritis (migratory), Chorea, Erythema marginatum, Subcutaneous nodules. Minor = fever, elevated ESR/CRP, prolonged PR, previous ARF. Need two major OR one major + two minor + strep evidence.

rows: Carditis, migratory arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
Fever, elevated ESR/CRP, prolonged PR interval, previous ARF/RHD
Monthly benzathine penicillin G (years to life depending on carditis)

questions.stem: A child with diarrhoea, anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated creatinine. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Post-infectious GN = nephritic syndrome (haematuria + hypertension + oliguria). NOT thrombocytopenia or haemolytic anaemia. No MAHA.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HUS = MAHA + THROMBOCYTOPENIA + AKI TRIAD. After diarrheal illness (typically E. coli O157:H7 producing Shiga toxin). Shiga toxin -> endothelial damage -> microthrombi -> schistocytes (MAHA) + thrombocytopenia + renal failure. Most common cause of AKI in children. Treatment: SUPPORTIVE (fluids, dialysis if needed). NO ANTIBIOTICS (increase Shiga toxin release). NO TMP-SMX.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TTP = MAHA + thrombocytopenia + NEUROLOGICAL symptoms + fever + renal impairment (pentad). ADAMTS13 deficiency. Adults >children. Treatment: PLASMA EXCHANGE. NOT the same as HUS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sepsis can cause thrombocytopenia and anaemia (DIC or haemolysis) but the SPECIFIC triad of MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI after diarrhoea = HUS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: DIC = ABNORMAL COAGULATION (prolonged PT + aPTT + low fibrinogen). TTP and HUS = NORMAL COAGULATION. This is the key differentiator.

questions.keyPoint: HUS TRIAD: MAHA + THROMBOCYTOPENIA + AKI. After STEC diarrhoea (E. coli O157:H7). Most common AKI cause in children. NO ANTIBIOTICS (worsen Shiga toxin release). COAGULATION IS NORMAL (vs DIC where coagulation is abnormal).

rows: TTP = adults + neurological + ADAMTS13 deficiency + plasma exchange
Antibiotics (especially TMP-SMX) increase Shiga toxin release and worsen HUS

questions.stem: LEUKOCYTOSIS WITH BLASTS A child with fatigue, bruising, joint pain, and recurrent infections. CBC shows anaemia, thrombocytopenia, leukocytosis with blasts. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALL = bone marrow infiltration by lymphoblasts -> ALL cell lines can be affected. Classic presentation: BONE PAIN + pallor + bruising + hepatosplenomegaly + lymphadenopathy + peripheral BLASTS. Leukocytosis with blasts on peripheral smear = LEUKAEMIA. Confirm with BMA (>20% blasts). Most common childhood cancer.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aplastic anaemia = pancytopenia with HYPOCELLULAR BM + NO BLASTS. No leukocytosis. Blasts on smear rules out aplastic anaemia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ITP = ISOLATED thrombocytopenia with NORMAL Hb and NORMAL WBC. Blasts on smear = NOT ITP.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MDS = dysplastic cells on BM with cytopenias. Rare in children. Blasts can be present but typically <20% in MDS (vs >20% in AML/ALL).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Lymphoma primarily affects lymph nodes (solid tumour). Peripheral blood involvement (blasts) = leukaemia. However, Burkitt lymphoma can present with BM involvement and look like leukaemia (Burkitt ALL = L3 ALL in old FAB classification).

questions.keyPoint: BLASTS on peripheral smear + bone pain + pallor + hepatosplenomegaly = LEUKAEMIA (ALL until proven otherwise in children). Confirm with BMA. Do NOT treat without BMA confirmation.

rows: Bone pain + pallor + blasts + hepatosplenomegaly + lymphadenopathy
t(12;21); age 1-9; B-cell ALL; low WBC

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy with pallor and failure to thrive. Blood film: spherocytes. Direct Coombs test NEGATIVE. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: AIHA = spherocytes + POSITIVE DAT (Coombs). Autoantibodies on RBC surface cause haemolysis. NEGATIVE DAT here RULES OUT AIHA.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis: spherocytes on smear + NEGATIVE DAT + family history + chronic haemolytic anaemia with intermittent jaundice and splenomegaly. DAT negative = NOT immune-mediated. Confirm: EMA fluorescence cytometry. Treat: folic acid + splenectomy (>6 years).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: G6PD: BITE CELLS (not spherocytes) + episodic haemolysis triggered by oxidant stress. X-linked. No spherocytes.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Thalassaemia: target cells + hypochromic microcytic anaemia + elevated HbA2/HbF on HPLC. Not spherocytes.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SCD: sickle cells on smear + clinical crises (bone pain, acute chest). Not spherocytes.

questions.keyPoint: SPHEROCYTES + NEGATIVE DAT = HEREDITARY SPHEROCYTOSIS. SPHEROCYTES + POSITIVE DAT = AIHA. This single test (DAT) differentiates these two conditions that both present with spherocytes.

rows: EMA fluorescence cytometry (replaced osmotic fragility test)
Gallstones, aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), haemolytic jaundice

questions.stem: URINE PROTEIN 4+ A 5-year-old with edema following viral infection. No haematuria, no hypertension. Low albumin, 4+ urine protein. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: PSGN = NEPHRITIC: haematuria + HYPERTENSION + oliguria + low C3. Normal BP and no haematuria here EXCLUDE PSGN.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Post-viral nephrotic syndrome in a child: NORMAL BP + NO haematuria + massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminaemia = MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE. Viral illness can trigger or worsen MCD. Treatment: prednisolone 2mg/kg/day. >90% response rate.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: HUS = MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI after diarrheal illness. No thrombocytopenia or AKI described. No massive proteinuria as the primary feature.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: IgA nephropathy = haematuria (macroscopic or microscopic) + mild proteinuria concurrent with infection. Massive proteinuria with NO haematuria is not typical of IgA.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SLE features needed: butterfly rash + ANA+ + anti-dsDNA+ + LOW C3/C4 + systemic features. No systemic features described in a 5-year-old. Normal complement in this presentation.

questions.keyPoint: NEPHROTIC in a child age 1-8: massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminaemia + NORMAL BP + NO haematuria = MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE. Treat empirically with prednisolone without biopsy in typical presentation.

questions.stem: A child with recurrent haemarthrosis. Maternal uncle has a bleeding tendency. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: VWD: MUCOSAL bleeding (epistaxis, bruising, gum bleeds). NOT typically haemarthrosis. Autosomal dominant (not X-linked). Maternal uncle having the same condition would be unusual for VWD (which affects both sexes equally).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HAEMARTHROSIS = PATHOGNOMONIC of haemophilia. X-LINKED RECESSIVE (maternal uncle = mother is carrier = son affected). Factor VIII deficiency -> prolonged aPTT + normal PT + normal platelets. Treatment: recombinant Factor VIII (rFVIII). Target joints from repeated haemarthrosis -> chronic arthropathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ITP: PETECHIAE + BRUISING from low platelets. NOT haemarthrosis. Deep joint bleeding = coagulation factor deficiency, not platelet problem.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DIC: acute life-threatening coagulopathy with both bleeding and clotting. Occurs in context of sepsis/obstetric emergency. Not a chronic condition causing recurrent haemarthrosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Factor VII deficiency: prolongs PT (extrinsic pathway). Haemophilia A = Factor VIII = intrinsic pathway = prolonged aPTT. Factor VII deficiency is rare.

questions.keyPoint: HAEMARTHROSIS + X-LINKED family history (maternal uncle affected) = HAEMOPHILIA A. X-linked recessive: maternal uncle affected -> mother is carrier -> 50% chance of affected sons. Prolonged aPTT, normal PT, normal platelets.

rows: Factor VIII (Haemophilia A); Factor IX (Haemophilia B)
Prolonged aPTT + NORMAL PT + NORMAL platelets
Recombinant Factor VIII (rFVIII) on demand or prophylaxis

questions.stem: A newborn with persistent jaundice, micropenis, and hypoglycaemia. Cortisol and GH are both low. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Congenital hypothyroidism: jaundice + poor feeding + constipation + large fontanelle + macroglossia + umbilical hernia + hypotonia + low T4 + high TSH. Does NOT cause micropenis or hypoglycaemia (hypoglycaemia from low GH/cortisol, not hypothyroidism).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypopituitarism (pituitary hormone deficiency): LOW GH + LOW ACTH (-> low cortisol) + low gonadotrophins (-> micropenis in males, as LH/FSH needed for fetal testosterone). Presents as: micropenis + hypoglycaemia (low GH + cortisol) + prolonged jaundice (from hypothyroidism from TSH deficiency). MRI: septo-optic dysplasia or absent pituitary stalk.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency): salt-wasting crisis (hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia) + ambiguous genitalia (females virilised). LOW cortisol + HIGH ACTH. Males with CAH have NORMAL or ENLARGED genitalia. NOT micropenis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sepsis: temperature instability + elevated inflammatory markers + positive blood culture. May cause hypoglycaemia secondarily but not micropenis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: IEM: metabolic acidosis + unusual odour + elevated plasma amino acids. Not the specific combination of micropenis + low GH + low cortisol.

questions.keyPoint: MICROPENIS + HYPOGLYCAEMIA + LOW GH + LOW CORTISOL in neonate = HYPOPITUITARISM. Micropenis = insufficient gonadotrophins (LH/FSH) for fetal testosterone production. MRI pituitary is essential.

rows: Pituitary hormone deficiency — GH, ACTH, TSH, LH/FSH deficiency
Micropenis + hypoglycaemia + prolonged jaundice + poor growth
Absent pituitary stalk, septo-optic dysplasia, or pituitary hypoplasia
Replace each deficient hormone: GH, hydrocortisone, levothyroxine, sex steroids

questions.stem: A 4-year-old with progressive headache and morning vomiting. MRI shows posterior fossa tumour. Most likely tumour type?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Most common MALIGNANT brain tumour in children. Posterior fossa. Morning headache + vomiting = RAISED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE from posterior fossa mass compressing 4th ventricle -> hydrocephalus. CSF seeding (drop metastases). PNET origin.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Suprasellar (NOT posterior fossa). Calcification + pituitary dysfunction + visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Can occur in posterior fossa (pilocytic astrocytoma = most common benign brain tumour). However, "most likely malignant tumour" in posterior fossa = medulloblastoma.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 4th ventricle in children. Causes obstructive hydrocephalus. Less common than medulloblastoma. Typically does NOT seed CSF as commonly as medulloblastoma.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pineal region tumours. Parinaud syndrome (upgaze palsy). Not posterior fossa (cerebellum).

questions.keyPoint: Morning headache + vomiting in a child = RAISED ICP until proven otherwise. MRI posterior fossa mass in a child = MEDULLOBLASTOMA as first diagnosis. Paediatric posterior fossa: medulloblastoma > pilocytic astrocytoma > ependymoma.

rows: Medulloblastoma — PNET, posterior fossa, CSF seeding
Classic sign of raised ICP (worse lying down overnight)

questions.stem: 9-MONTH-OLD | WEIGHT LOSS | DIARRHOEA | FOOD INTOLERANCE AFTER STARTING SOLIDS | ANTI-tTG IgA ELEVATED A 9-month-old with weight loss, diarrhoea, and food intolerance after starting solids. Anti-tTG IgA is high. Best long-term management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: COELIAC DISEASE (gluten-sensitive enteropathy): immune-mediated damage to small bowel villi triggered by GLUTEN (wheat, barley, rye). Anti-tTG IgA is the best screening test. Diagnosis confirmed with duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia + increased intraepithelial lymphocytes). Treatment: LIFELONG STRICT GLUTEN-FREE DIET. Removes the trigger -> allows mucosal healing.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lactose intolerance can be SECONDARY to coeliac disease (damaged villi cannot produce lactase). However, the PRIMARY treatment is gluten-free diet, not lactose-free. After healing on GFD, lactose tolerance often returns.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids are NOT first-line for coeliac disease. Used only for refractory coeliac disease (not responding to GFD). Primary treatment = GFD.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Elemental formula may be used temporarily in severely malnourished children with coeliac. NOT the long-term management. Gluten-free diet is the definitive treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Coeliac disease is IMMUNE-MEDIATED, not infectious. Antibiotics have NO role.

questions.keyPoint: COELIAC DISEASE = anti-tTG IgA elevated + villous atrophy on biopsy. TREATMENT: LIFELONG GLUTEN-FREE DIET. Avoid wheat, barley, rye. Oats: controversial (usually safe unless contaminated). Strict adherence prevents lymphoma risk.

rows: Duodenal biopsy: villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia + IEL increase
Wheat, barley, rye (BROW mnemonic: Barley, Rye, Oats-maybe, Wheat)

questions.stem: A 12-hour-old neonate has rapidly rising jaundice. Mother is O+, baby is A+. Underlying pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Physiological jaundice appears after 24 HOURS of life (not within 12 hours). ANY jaundice in the FIRST 24 HOURS is PATHOLOGICAL. This baby has jaundice at 12 hours = pathological.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Rh incompatibility: Rh-NEGATIVE mother + Rh-POSITIVE baby. Mother here is O+ (Rh POSITIVE). Rh incompatibility requires an Rh-negative mother. Excluded here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: O+ mother has naturally occurring IgG antibodies against A and B antigens (from prior food/blood exposure). These cross the placenta -> haemolyse fetal/neonatal A+ RBCs. MOST COMMON cause of haemolytic disease of the newborn. Jaundice appears in FIRST 24 HOURS. Positive Coombs test. Treatment: phototherapy, occasionally exchange transfusion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Breastfeeding jaundice: appears in FIRST 3-4 DAYS from insufficient milk intake. NOT within 12 hours. INDIRECT bilirubin. No haemolytic cause.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Neonatal sepsis can cause jaundice, haemolysis, and other features. However, the clinical context of O+ mother + A+ baby + jaundice at 12 hours = ABO incompatibility specifically.

questions.keyPoint: JAUNDICE IN FIRST 24 HOURS = ALWAYS PATHOLOGICAL. O+ mother + A+ baby + jaundice at 12 hours = ABO INCOMPATIBILITY. Most common haemolytic disease of the newborn. Rh incompatibility: Rh-NEGATIVE mother needed.

rows: ALWAYS pathological — haemolytic cause until proven otherwise
O mother + A or B baby; IgG anti-A or anti-B crosses placenta
Rh-NEGATIVE mother required; now rare with Anti-D prophylaxis

questions.stem: A 6-month-old with brief symmetric flexor spasms in clusters. EEG shows hypsarrhythmia. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Absence: 3-8 year olds + brief staring (5-30sec) + 3Hz spike-wave on EEG. Not infantile flexor spasms.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Myoclonic = sudden brief jerks of limbs/body. Different pattern. No clusters of flexor spasms.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: WEST SYNDROME = infantile spasms + hypsarrhythmia on EEG + developmental regression. Clusters of flexor spasms (Salaam attacks). Onset 3-12 months. Treatment: ACTH or vigabatrin. If TSC-related: vigabatrin first-line. Hypsarrhythmia = chaotic high-voltage EEG = PATHOGNOMONIC.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Febrile seizures occur with fever >38C in children 6 months to 5 years. No association with clusters of spasms or hypsarrhythmia on EEG. Not in afebrile infants at 6 months.

questions.keyPoint: WEST SYNDROME = infantile spasms + HYPSARRHYTHMIA on EEG + developmental regression. Salaam attacks (clusters of flexor spasms). Onset 3-12 months. ACTH = first-line.

questions.stem: A preterm neonate develops respiratory distress within hours of birth. CXR shows ground-glass opacities and air bronchograms. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Preterm + surfactant deficiency + ground-glass + air bronchograms = RDS. Exogenous surfactant + CPAP = treatment. Antenatal steroids = prevention.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Consolidation on CXR + risk factors (PROM, maternal fever). Not ground-glass pattern specifically.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Post-term + meconium-stained fluid + hyperinflation + patchy asymmetric. Not preterm.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Term/near-term + C-section + perihilar streaky markings. Resolves in 24-72 hours.

questions.keyPoint: PRETERM + ground-glass + air bronchograms = NEONATAL RDS (surfactant deficiency). Antenatal steroids prevent; postnatal exogenous surfactant treats.

rows: Exogenous surfactant (INSURE: INtubate, SURfactant, Extubate to CPAP)

questions.stem: A 30-week preterm with recurrent apnoea and bradycardia. Sepsis screen negative. Most appropriate initial therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MV is reserved for SEVERE apnoea or respiratory failure not responding to caffeine. Over-intervention for initial management of apnoea of prematurity.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antibiotics treat SEPSIS (which can cause apnoea). Sepsis screen here is NEGATIVE — antibiotics not indicated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Antenatal steroids prevent RDS. Postnatal steroids used for BPD. NOT for apnoea of prematurity.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: APNOEA OF PREMATURITY = immature respiratory centre. Treatment: CAFFEINE CITRATE (methylxanthine) = central respiratory stimulant. Stimulates medullary respiratory centre. Reduces apnoeas + bradycardias + need for mechanical ventilation. Loading dose then daily maintenance. Most effective pharmacological treatment.

questions.keyPoint: APNOEA OF PREMATURITY = CAFFEINE CITRATE (methylxanthine) first-line. Stimulates central respiratory drive. Reduces need for mechanical ventilation. Standard of care in premature neonates.

rows: Caffeine = methylxanthine = adenosine antagonist = stimulates respiratory centre
Reduces apnoeas, BPD, ROP, neurodevelopmental impairment

questions.stem: A child presents with fever, bone pain, and hepatosplenomegaly. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ITP: isolated thrombocytopenia + petechiae. No bone pain or hepatosplenomegaly.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BONE PAIN + fever + hepatosplenomegaly + lymphadenopathy in a child = LEUKAEMIA until proven otherwise. Bone pain from BM infiltration. Hepatosplenomegaly from leukaemic infiltration + extramedullary haemopoiesis. Confirm with BMA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Thalassaemia major: hepatosplenomegaly + anaemia from 6 months. However, no fever or bone pain as primary features. Thalassaemia facies + high HbF/HbA2 on HPLC.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Aplastic anaemia: pancytopenia + hypocellular BM + NO hepatosplenomegaly or bone pain.

questions.keyPoint: BONE PAIN + fever + hepatosplenomegaly in a child = LEUKAEMIA until proven otherwise. Do not delay bone marrow aspiration.

rows: Bone pain + pallor + hepatosplenomegaly + lymphadenopathy + blasts

questions.stem: A child with pallor and spoon-shaped nails (koilonychia). Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Megaloblastic (B12/folate): macrocytic anaemia + hypersegmented neutrophils + neurological (B12). No koilonychia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: KOILONYCHIA (spoon-shaped nails) = PATHOGNOMONIC of iron deficiency anaemia. Other IDA signs: pallor, angular stomatitis, glossitis, pica, fatigue, tachycardia. Blood film: hypochromic microcytic.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Aplastic: pancytopenia + hypocellular BM. No koilonychia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SCD: normocytic anaemia + sickle cells on smear + painful crises. No koilonychia.

questions.keyPoint: KOILONYCHIA (spoon nails) = PATHOGNOMONIC of iron deficiency anaemia. Other iron-deficiency signs: pica, angular stomatitis, glossitis, pallor, fatigue.

rows: Low ferritin, low serum iron, HIGH TIBC

questions.stem: A child develops periorbital edema and proteinuria following a viral infection. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IgA nephropathy = haematuria concurrent with infection. Massive proteinuria + edema + normal BP is not typical of IgA.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PSGN = nephritic: haematuria + hypertension + low C3. Edema + proteinuria + normal BP = nephrotic = MCD.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Post-viral nephrotic syndrome in children (periorbital edema + proteinuria + no haematuria + normal BP) = MCD. Viral infections can trigger MCD relapses. Treat with prednisolone.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Requires SLE features + ANA+ + anti-dsDNA+ + low C3/C4. Not in a child without systemic features.

questions.keyPoint: Post-viral nephrotic syndrome (edema + proteinuria + normal BP + no haematuria) in a child = MCD. Treat with prednisolone empirically.

rows: Nephrotic: proteinuria + low albumin + oedema + normal BP. Nephritic: haematuria + hypertension + low GFR

questions.stem: A 4-year-old with sandpaper-like rash, strawberry tongue, and recent sore throat. Causative organism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: H. influenzae type b: epiglottitis, meningitis, pneumonia. Does NOT cause scarlet fever rash.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: S. aureus: SSSS (scalded skin syndrome) = generalised skin peeling/bullae. NOT sandpaper rash or strawberry tongue.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mycoplasma: "walking pneumonia" + cold agglutinins + erythema multiforme rash. NOT scarlet fever.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SCARLET FEVER = Group A Streptococcus (S. pyogenes) producing erythrogenic toxin (SPE toxins). Classic: sandpaper rash + STRAWBERRY TONGUE + flushed face with perioral pallor + fever + sore throat. Treatment: penicillin 10 days. Can lead to ARF if untreated.

questions.keyPoint: SCARLET FEVER = GAS (Streptococcus pyogenes) erythrogenic toxin. Sandpaper rash + strawberry tongue + perioral pallor. Treat with PENICILLIN 10 days. Prevent ARF and PSGN.

rows: Sandpaper rash + strawberry tongue + flushed face + perioral pallor
Acute rheumatic fever + PSGN if untreated

questions.stem: Deficiency in which vitamin is associated with neural tube defects?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Vitamin A deficiency: xerophthalmia, night blindness, increased infection susceptibility. Excess Vitamin A is TERATOGENIC (craniosynostosis, heart defects). NOT neural tube defects.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency: megaloblastic anaemia + subacute combined degeneration of the cord (neurological). B12 deficiency can IMPAIR folate metabolism (methyl trap), contributing to NTD indirectly, but the PRIMARY cause is folic acid deficiency.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: FOLIC ACID deficiency during periconceptional period = neural tube defects (spina bifida, anencephaly, encephalocele). Folate is essential for neural tube closure (days 21-28 of gestation — often before pregnancy is known). Supplementation: 400 mcg/day for all women planning pregnancy + first trimester. High-risk women (previous NTD, antiepileptics): 5mg/day.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Zinc deficiency: impaired growth, immune function, wound healing, skin lesions. Not specifically neural tube defects.

questions.keyPoint: FOLIC ACID deficiency = NEURAL TUBE DEFECTS (spina bifida, anencephaly). Take 400mcg/day from at least 1 month before conception. Valproate depletes folate — always supplement.

rows: 400 mcg/day starting 1 month before conception + throughout first trimester
Depletes folate + direct teratogen for NTDs. Always supplement

questions.topic: Cow's Milk Protein Allergy vs NEC
questions.stem: A child switched from breastfeeding to cow's milk develops bloody diarrhoea and abdominal distension. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Intussusception: 6-24 months + colicky pain + currant-jelly stools + palpable mass. Triggered by Peyer's patch hypertrophy (post-viral). Not specifically by cow's milk introduction.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NEC primarily affects preterm neonates. Formula feeding (vs breastfeeding) is a risk factor. If this is a preterm infant being switched to formula = NEC is possible. Treat: NPO + antibiotics + surgical resection for necrosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Appendicitis: >5 years + periumbilical migrating to RIF pain + rebound tenderness + Rovsing's sign. Not associated with cow's milk.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Meckel diverticulum: painless rectal bleeding (from ectopic gastric mucosa) OR intestinal obstruction. Not specifically triggered by cow's milk introduction.

questions.keyPoint: In a PRETERM neonate: switch from breastmilk to formula = NEC risk. In a TERM infant/toddler: switch from breastmilk to cow's milk + bloody diarrhoea = COW'S MILK PROTEIN ALLERGY (CMPA). Context determines diagnosis.

rows: Preterm; pneumatosis intestinalis; formula feeding risk factor
Term infant; bloody diarrhoea/mucus after cow's milk; manage with hydrolysed formula or amino acid formula
NPO + NG decompression + IV antibiotics + surgery
Switch to extensively hydrolysed or amino acid formula

questions.stem: Which congenital infection is most commonly associated with sensorineural hearing loss?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ongenital syphilis: Hutchinson triad (interstitial keratitis + SNHL + Hutchinson teeth). SNHL occurs but less ommon than CMV. Saddle nose + rhagades.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ongenital toxoplasmosis: classic triad = hydrocephalus + chorioretinitis + intracranial calcifications. SNHL is less rominent.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ongenital rubella: PDA/PS + cataracts + SNHL + microcephaly + blueberry muffin rash. SNHL does occur. owever, CMV is MORE COMMON overall as a cause of non-hereditary SNHL.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MV (Cytomegalovirus) = MOST COMMON cause of non-hereditary sensorineural hearing loss in children. ongenital CMV: most infected neonates are asymptomatic at birth but 10-15% develop late-onset SNHL. eatures: periventricular calcifications + microcephaly + hepatosplenomegaly + petechiae. Diagnosis: CMV DNA n urine/saliva in first 3 weeks of life. Treatment: IV ganciclovir -> oral valganciclovir for 6 months (reduces SNHL rogression).

questions.keyPoint: CMV = MOST COMMON cause of non-hereditary sensorineural hearing loss in children. Most neonates are asymptomatic at birth. Late-onset SNHL can develop. Treat with valganciclovir for 6 months to prevent progression.

rows: Also causes SNHL but less common than CMV overall
Hutchinson triad: SNHL + interstitial keratitis + Hutchinson teeth

questions.topic: Transposition of Great Arteries — Egg on String CXR
questions.stem: A newborn with severe central cyanosis not improving with O2. CXR shows "egg on a string" appearance. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TAPVR = pulmonary veins drain anomalously to right side. CXR: "snowman" or "figure of 8" (supracardiac TAPVR with vertical vein). Not egg on string.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TGA = aorta from RV + PA from LV = parallel circulation. SEVERE CYANOSIS FROM BIRTH. O2 does NOT help. "EGG ON A STRING" (or egg on its side) = PATHOGNOMONIC CXR of TGA. Narrow mediastinum (vessels overlapping anteroposteriorly) + enlarged heart = egg shape. PGE1 (prostaglandin E1) to keep PDA open for mixing. Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy. Definitive: arterial switch (Jatene procedure).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TOF: BOOT-SHAPED HEART on CXR (right ventricular hypertrophy + pulmonary artery hypoplasia). Right-sided aortic arch in 25%. Not egg on string.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tricuspid atresia: absence of tricuspid valve + small RV. CXR: decreased pulmonary vascular markings. Typical signs include left axis deviation on ECG (unusual for congenital heart disease).

questions.keyPoint: EGG ON A STRING CXR = TGA (transposition of great arteries). Narrow mediastinum (vessels in AP alignment = narrow shadow) + globular heart = egg appearance. EMERGENCY: PGE1 then Rashkind then arterial switch.

rows: Egg on a string = narrow mediastinum + globular heart
Balloon atrial septostomy to create ASD for mixing
Arterial switch operation (Jatene) within first 2 weeks of life

questions.stem: A newborn has a continuous "machine-like" murmur. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ASD = ejection systolic murmur + fixed split S2. NOT continuous murmur.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: VSD = PANSYSTOLIC murmur at LLSB. Not continuous.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CONTINUOUS MACHINERY MURMUR at left clavicle/upper LSB = PDA (patent ductus arteriosus). The murmur is continuous because flow occurs throughout both systole and diastole (aortic pressure always exceeds pulmonary pressure). Bounding pulses + wide pulse pressure. Treatment: indomethacin (preterm), surgical/device closure.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TOF = ejection systolic murmur (pulmonary stenosis component). Not continuous.

questions.keyPoint: CONTINUOUS MACHINERY MURMUR = PDA. Only condition in paediatric cardiology with a truly continuous murmur (flows in both systole and diastole). Bounding pulses = wide pulse pressure from aortic run-off.

questions.stem: RETRACTIONS A 6-month-old with 2-day cough, wheezing, respiratory distress, nasal flaring, and intercostal retractions. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: RSV BRONCHIOLITIS: infant <2 years + wheezing + respiratory distress + nasal flaring + retractions + poor feeding. First episode of wheezing in infant = bronchiolitis. Treatment = SUPPORTIVE: O2 + hydration + suctioning. NO bronchodilators, NO steroids.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pneumonia: fever + productive cough + consolidation on CXR + focal signs. Not typically wheeze-predominant in infants.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Croup: barking cough + INSPIRATORY stridor (not wheezing). Subglottic, not bronchiolar.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Asthma: recurrent episodes, family history, atopy, usually >2 years. FIRST EPISODE OF WHEEZE in an infant = bronchiolitis, not asthma.

questions.keyPoint: BRONCHIOLITIS = first-episode wheeze in infant <2 years. RSV most common cause. SUPPORTIVE ONLY. No bronchodilators (not evidence-based). No steroids. Admit if SpO2 <92% or unable to feed.

rows: Supportive: O2 if SpO2 <92% + hydration + nasal suctioning

questions.stem: An infant with headache and neck stiffness. CSF: cloudy, neutrophilia, low glucose, high protein. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fungal (Cryptococcal): lymphocytic + low glucose + high protein + opening pressure very high. India ink stain positive. Immunocompromised patients.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Viral (aseptic) meningitis: LYMPHOCYTIC CSF + normal/slightly low glucose + normal/mildly high protein. CLEAR CSF. Generally benign and self-limiting.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: NEUTROPHILIC CSF + VERY LOW glucose (bacteria consume glucose) + HIGH protein (inflammation + disrupted BBB) = BACTERIAL MENINGITIS. Cloudy/turbid CSF = bacterial (vs clear = viral). Treat immediately: IV ceftriaxone + dexamethasone.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TB meningitis: LYMPHOCYTIC (subacute) + low glucose + very high protein + high opening pressure + fibrin web on standing. Subacute presentation (weeks). AFB on ZN stain.

questions.keyPoint: BACTERIAL MENINGITIS CSF: NEUTROPHILIC + LOW glucose + HIGH protein + CLOUDY. Treatment: IV ceftriaxone + IV dexamethasone IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT wait for results.

rows: Clear; lymphocytosis; glucose normal/slightly low; protein normal/slightly high
Clear; lymphocytosis; very low glucose; very high protein; AFB
Clear; lymphocytosis; very high protein; India ink positive

questions.stem: CHILD | BRUISING + GENERALISED PETECHIAE AFTER VIRAL INFECTION | PLATELETS 15,000/uL A child develops bruising and generalised petechiae after a viral infection. Platelets 15,000/uL. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Post-viral ISOLATED THROMBOCYTOPENIA + petechiae/bruising + otherwise normal CBC = ITP. Auto-antibodies (IgG) against GPIIb/IIIa triggered by viral illness. Management: IVIG or steroids if <20,000 or active bleeding. >80% self-resolve.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Haemophilia A: Factor VIII deficiency + haemarthrosis + normal platelets. NOT thrombocytopenia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: VWD: mucosal bleeding + prolonged aPTT + NORMAL PLATELET COUNT. Platelet count is NORMAL in VWD (only function is impaired).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Aplastic anaemia: PANCYTOPENIA (all three cell lines reduced). Here only platelets are reduced (isolated thrombocytopenia) = not aplastic anaemia.

questions.keyPoint: POST-VIRAL ISOLATED THROMBOCYTOPENIA + petechiae + otherwise normal CBC = ITP. The word "isolated" is key — aplastic anaemia has pancytopenia (all cell lines reduced).

rows: IVIG or prednisolone if <20,000 or active bleeding

questions.stem: A child with pneumonia and lobar consolidation. Most common causative organism in CAP?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Mycoplasma = "walking pneumonia" (child appears well despite infiltrates). INTERSTITIAL pattern on CXR. Most common in school-age children and adolescents. Not the most common overall cause of CAP. Treat: macrolide (azithromycin).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: S. aureus pneumonia: severe, rapidly progressive + cavitation + pleural empyema. Risk: post-influenza. Not the most common CAP cause.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: C. pneumoniae: school-age children + adolescents + adults. Interstitial infiltrates. Less common than S. pneumoniae. Treat: macrolide.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae = MOST COMMON cause of community-acquired pneumonia in ALL age groups including children. LOBAR consolidation is the classic CXR pattern. Rust-colored sputum. Treatment: amoxicillin (if susceptible) or ceftriaxone.

questions.keyPoint: MOST COMMON CAP in all ages = S. PNEUMONIAE. Lobar consolidation on CXR. Treat: amoxicillin (first-line for mild CAP in children). Macrolide if atypical (Mycoplasma) suspected.

rows: Amoxicillin (mild) or ceftriaxone (severe CAP in children)

questions.stem: A child develops tea-colored urine following a streptococcal infection. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TEA-COLORED (cola-colored) URINE = haematuria. Post-streptococcal + haematuria + hypertension + oedema = PSGN. Nephritic syndrome. Low C3. >95% resolve spontaneously in children.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MCD = nephrotic syndrome: frothy urine (proteinuria), oedema, no haematuria, normal BP. NOT tea-colored urine (haematuria).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: IgA nephropathy = haematuria concurrent with infection (<5 days). PSGN = 1-3 WEEK latent period. This post-streptococcal + latency = PSGN.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: SLE features required. No systemic features described here.

questions.keyPoint: TEA-COLORED URINE = haematuria = NEPHRITIC SYNDROME. Post-streptococcal + haematuria + hypertension = PSGN. Low C3, normal C4. >95% resolve spontaneously.

questions.stem: A child with polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hyperthyroidism: weight loss + polydipsia possible BUT also tachycardia + heat intolerance + exophthalmos + goitre + diarrhoea. Not the primary cause of classic polyuria-polydipsia-weight loss triad.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CRF can cause polyuria (inability to concentrate urine). But CRF in a child = hypertension + oedema + failure to thrive + specific renal history. Not isolated polyuria-polydipsia-weight loss.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: DI also causes polyuria + polydipsia. However, in DI there is NO weight loss from catabolism and glucose is NORMAL. DI = water problem; DM = glucose problem.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: POLYURIA + POLYDIPSIA + WEIGHT LOSS = CLASSIC T1DM triad. Insulin deficiency -> hyperglycaemia -> osmotic diuresis -> polyuria -> compensatory polydipsia. WEIGHT LOSS from catabolism (fat + muscle breakdown). Diagnose: random glucose >11.1 mmol/L or FPG >7 mmol/L.

questions.keyPoint: POLYURIA + POLYDIPSIA + WEIGHT LOSS = T1DM until proven otherwise in children. Check random glucose (>11.1 mmol/L with symptoms = diagnostic). DKA can be presentation.

rows: Hyperglycaemia -> glucose exceeds renal threshold -> glycosuria -> osmotic diuresis
Random glucose >11.1 mmol/L with symptoms OR FPG >7.0 mmol/L twice
Insulin (no other medication is adequate for T1DM)

questions.stem: A child with severe dehydration from diarrhoea. First thing to do?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ORS (oral rehydration salts) = MILD-MODERATE dehydration (5-10%). SEVERE dehydration = cannot take oral fluids effectively + circulatory compromise = IV fluids.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antidiarrheals (loperamide) are CONTRAINDICATED in children. Can cause toxic megacolon and respiratory depression. Never use in paediatric gastroenteritis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SEVERE DEHYDRATION (>10% body weight loss, sunken eyes, no tears, dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, lethargy, absent pulse/capillary refill >3 sec, shock) = IV FLUID RESUSCITATION IMMEDIATELY. WHO/IMCI protocol: Ringer's lactate 100mL/kg over 3 hours (infants) or 30mL/kg in 30 mins (shock). ORS is for mild-moderate dehydration only.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Antibiotics only if bacterial dysentery suspected (bloody diarrhoea + fever + specific epidemiology). Most paediatric gastroenteritis is viral. Dehydration management FIRST.

questions.keyPoint: SEVERE DEHYDRATION = IV FLUIDS IMMEDIATELY. ORS for mild-moderate. IV for severe (>10% dehydration, signs of shock). NEVER antidiarrheals in children.

questions.stem: FISSURES A newborn has generalised hypotonia, single palmar crease, and up-slanting palpebral fissures. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Trisomy 18: ROCKER-BOTTOM feet + clenched fists (overlapping fingers) + micrognathia + severe intellectual disability. Fatal within 1 year. Not simian crease.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: 45,X0: short stature + webbed neck + coarctation + primary amenorrhoea + streak gonads. Female phenotype. Not in a newborn with hypotonia + simian crease.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Trisomy 21: up-slanting palpebral fissures + epicanthal folds + flat face + macroglossia + SINGLE PALMAR CREASE (simian crease) + sandal gap + small ears + hypotonia (neonatal). Associated: ASD/VSD, Hirschsprung, leukaemia (ALL/AML), hypothyroidism, Alzheimer's (early onset).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Trisomy 13: midline defects (holoprosencephaly) + polydactyly + cleft lip/palate + microphthalmia. Fatal within 1 month. Not simian crease.

questions.keyPoint: DOWN SYNDROME (Trisomy 21): up-slanting palpebral fissures + flat face + simian crease + hypotonia + ASD/VSD. SIMIAN CREASE = single palmar crease (not exclusive to Down but classic association).

rows: Up-slanting palpebral fissures + flat face + macroglossia + simian crease + hypotonia
NIPT + nuchal translucency + combined first trimester screen

questions.stem: A 2-year-old presents with severe bone pain and dactylitis. Blood smear shows sickle-shaped erythrocytes. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Thalassaemia major: target cells + hypochromic microcytic + raised HbF/HbA2. No sickle cells. Symptoms from 6 months (when HbF replaced by HbS or absent HbA).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HS: spherocytes + negative DAT + splenomegaly. Not sickle cells.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SICKLE CELLS on blood smear + dactylitis (hand-foot syndrome = first painful crisis) + bone pain = SICKLE CELL DISEASE. Dactylitis in a 2-year-old = first vaso-occlusive crisis. Confirm: HbS on HPLC. Manage pain crisis: IV fluids + analgesia + O2.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: G6PD: bite cells + Heinz bodies + episodic triggered haemolysis. Not sickle cells.

questions.keyPoint: DACTYLITIS (painful swollen hands/feet) = FIRST VASO-OCCLUSIVE CRISIS of SCD in infants/toddlers. Sickle cells on smear. Autosomal recessive. Confirm with Hb electrophoresis/HPLC (HbSS).

rows: Hand-foot syndrome = first SCD crisis in toddlers (most common <4y)

questions.stem: INFECTION | FLACCID PARALYSIS A 5-year-old has progressive ascending symmetrical weakness, absent DTR, and recent respiratory infection. Now flaccid paralysis. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GBS: ASCENDING FLACCID PARALYSIS + ABSENT DTR + post-infectious (respiratory or GI). Classic Miller Fisher variant: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia. LP: albuminocytological dissociation. NCS: demyelinating. Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis. NO STEROIDS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CP: non-progressive static brain injury. SPASTIC not flaccid. DTRs BRISK (UMN). Not ascending weakness.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MS: rare in children. Relapsing-remitting neurological symptoms separated in time and space. Not acute ascending paralysis post-infection.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Transverse myelitis: sudden-onset weakness/sensory loss at a SPECIFIC SPINAL LEVEL. Bilateral. Sphincter involvement. Not progressive ascending. MRI shows cord lesion at one level.

questions.keyPoint: GBS: ASCENDING flaccid paralysis + ABSENT DTR + post-infectious. LP = albuminocytological dissociation. Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis. STEROIDS = CONTRAINDICATED.

rows: Ascending flaccid paralysis starting in legs + ABSENT DTR
Campylobacter jejuni most common; also Mycoplasma, EBV, CMV, CMV, influenza vaccine
Albuminocytological dissociation: HIGH protein + NORMAL cells

questions.stem: CHILD | CHRONIC DIARRHEA | ABDOMINAL BLOATING | FAILURE TO THRIVE | ANTI-tTG IgA POSITIVE A child with chronic diarrhoea, abdominal bloating, and failure to thrive. Anti-tTG IgA is positive. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: UC: bloody diarrhoea + rectal bleeding + mucus. Affects colon only. Anti-tTG IgA negative. Biopsy: continuous crypt inflammation + goblet cell depletion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Crohn's: can affect mouth to anus. Skip lesions. Non-caseating granulomas. Perianal disease. Anti-tTG IgA negative. Not specific for coeliac.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ANTI-tTG IgA positive + chronic diarrhoea + abdominal bloating + failure to thrive = COELIAC DISEASE. Gluten-sensitive enteropathy. Villous atrophy on duodenal biopsy. Treatment: lifelong gluten-free diet.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: IBS: functional GI disorder. No weight loss or failure to thrive. No antibodies. Normal investigations.

questions.keyPoint: COELIAC DISEASE: anti-tTG IgA + failure to thrive + chronic diarrhoea + bloating. CONFIRM with duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia + increased intraepithelial lymphocytes). TREAT: lifelong gluten-free diet.

rows: Duodenal biopsy: villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia
Lymphoma, osteoporosis, infertility, IDA, dermatitis herpetiformis

questions.stem: In a child with Kawasaki disease features, what is the most important investigation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ECHO is the MOST IMPORTANT investigation in Kawasaki disease. Detects CORONARY ARTERY ANEURYSMS (the major life-threatening complication). Echo must be performed at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6-8 weeks regardless of whether criteria are met. Also evaluates myocardial function.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ESR/CRP are elevated in Kawasaki (minor criteria) and help support the diagnosis. However, they do NOT detect coronary aneurysms — the critical complication that determines long-term prognosis and treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Same as ESR — elevated but not the most important investigation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: FBC shows leucocytosis and thrombocytosis (phase 2). Important but not the most critical investigation for management.

questions.keyPoint: MOST IMPORTANT INVESTIGATION IN KAWASAKI = ECHOCARDIOGRAM. Detects coronary artery aneurysms. Must be done at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6-8 weeks even if features resolve. Echo guides management and anticoagulation decisions.

questions.stem: In a child with PSGN (post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis), what is the characteristic finding on electron microscopy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Mesangial IgA = IgA NEPHROPATHY (Berger's disease). NOT PSGN. Key differentiator: IgA nephropathy has mesangial IgA deposits; PSGN has subepithelial IgG + C3 deposits.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Subepithelial hump-like immune complex deposits (IgG + C3)
choices.explanation: PSGN on EM = SUBEPITHELIAL "HUMP-LIKE" IMMUNE COMPLEX DEPOSITS. Pathognomonic. Activated complement C3 deposits subepithelially. LM: diffuse proliferative GN. IF: "starry sky" pattern of granular IgG + C3 deposits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal appearance on light microscopy (minimal change pattern)
choices.explanation: CD on LM = NORMAL. MCD on EM = podocyte foot process effacement. NOT PSGN.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: rescentic GN = rapidly progressive GN (RPGN) with fibrocellular crescents on LM. Represents ggressive/necrotising GN. NOT typical PSGN picture (though can complicate severe PSGN).

questions.keyPoint: PSGN on EM = SUBEPITHELIAL "HUMP-LIKE" IMMUNE COMPLEX DEPOSITS. These are IgG + C3 complexes deposited between GBM and podocytes. LM: diffuse proliferative GN. IF: "starry sky" granular IgG + C3.

rows: Subepithelial hump-like immune complex deposits (IgG + C3)
Granular IgG + C3 in a "starry sky" pattern
Podocyte foot process effacement; LM normal
Subepithelial deposits along GBM — "spike and dome"

questions.stem: A baby develops high fever for 3-4 days. Fever suddenly resolves and a rash appears. Causative organism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ROSEOLA INFANTUM = 6th disease = HHV-6 (Human Herpesvirus 6). Classic: high fever (38.9-40.5C) for 3-5 days -> fever RESOLVES -> RASH APPEARS (rose-colored macular rash on trunk). Affects infants 6-18 months. Benign, self-limiting. Can cause febrile seizures during high fever phase.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Parainfluenza virus: CROUP (barking cough + stridor). No fever-then-rash sequence.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Measles: cough + conjunctivitis + Koplik spots -> CONCURRENT rash (not after fever resolution). Descending maculopapular rash. MMR-preventable.

questions.keyPoint: FEVER 3-4 DAYS then FEVER RESOLVES then RASH APPEARS = ROSEOLA INFANTUM (HHV-6). Classic "fever then rash" sequence is the diagnostic clue. Affects 6-18 month infants. Benign.

rows: High fever 3-5 days -> fever resolves -> rose-colored rash on trunk

questions.stem: A child presents with drooling, tripod position, and appears toxic. Management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: EPIGLOTTITIS: drooling + tripod position + toxic appearance + stridor = life-threatening airway emergency. PROTOCOL: (1) Keep child calm + upright (2) Call anaesthetist IMMEDIATELY (3) Take to THEATRE under controlled conditions (4) Intubate with backup cricothyrotomy available (5) IV antibiotics after airway secured.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Examining the throat or performing laryngoscopy in emergency department CAN CAUSE COMPLETE AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION AND DEATH. The supraglottic oedema is fragile — any stimulation can trigger complete closure. Always take to theatre.

questions.keyPoint: EPIGLOTTITIS RULE: NEVER examine throat, NEVER put supine, NEVER distress the child. Call anaesthetist -> Theatre -> Intubate under controlled conditions -> IV ceftriaxone after airway secured.

rows: Examine throat, put supine, distress child, leave alone
Keep calm + upright -> anaesthetist -> theatre -> intubate -> ceftriaxone
Thumb sign (swollen epiglottis on lateral neck X-ray)
H. influenzae type b (classic); also S. aureus post-Hib vaccine era

questions.stem: A child with "walking pneumonia" (appears well despite chest symptoms and infiltrates). Treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MYCOPLASMA PNEUMONIAE = "Walking pneumonia" (child appears relatively well despite pneumonia). ATYPICAL organism = NO CELL WALL -> beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins) are INEFFECTIVE. Treatment: MACROLIDES (azithromycin, clarithromycin) OR doxycycline (>8 years). Azithromycin: 5-day course.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Clindamycin: covers anaerobes + Gram-positive. Clindamycin inhibits protein synthesis (50S ribosomal subunit) and covers anaerobes and Gram-positives. However, it has poor intrinsic activity against Mycoplasma pneumoniae. IMPORTANT: Do not confuse — it is beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins) that fail because Mycoplasma has no cell wall. Clindamycin fails for different reasons (intrinsic resistance pattern). Macrolides and tetracyclines are effective.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cephalosporins: INEFFECTIVE for Mycoplasma (no cell wall to target). Used for S. pneumoniae lobar pneumonia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Vancomycin: Gram-positive bacteria (MRSA, C. difficile). Completely ineffective against Mycoplasma.

questions.keyPoint: WALKING PNEUMONIA = Mycoplasma pneumoniae. ATYPICAL ORGANISM = no cell wall = beta-lactams INEFFECTIVE. Treat with MACROLIDE (azithromycin). Other atypicals: Chlamydia pneumoniae, Legionella -> all need macrolide.

rows: "Walking pneumonia" — child appears well despite infiltrates
Interstitial/reticular pattern; out of proportion to clinical severity
For children >8 years — alternative to macrolide

questions.stem: A child with features of rheumatic fever (URTI + high ASO titer). What is the ongoing secondary prophylaxis management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SECONDARY PROPHYLAXIS for ARF = MONTHLY INTRAMUSCULAR BENZATHINE PENICILLIN G (1.2 million units). Duration depends on severity: no carditis = 5 years or until 21 years (whichever longer); carditis with resolved valvular disease = 10 years or until 21; persistent valvular disease = lifelong. Monthly IM penicillin = most effective (eliminates compliance issues).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Oral amoxicillin/phenoxymethylpenicillin twice daily is an ALTERNATIVE for secondary prophylaxis but INFERIOR to monthly IM penicillin due to compliance issues. Not first-line.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Macrolides are used only for penicillin-ALLERGIC patients. Not weekly — if used, given daily. Not first-line.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Secondary prophylaxis is ESSENTIAL to prevent recurrent strep pharyngitis -> recurrent ARF -> worsening valvular damage. Without prophylaxis, strep recurrence can cause progressive rheumatic heart disease.

questions.keyPoint: ARF SECONDARY PROPHYLAXIS = monthly IM BENZATHINE PENICILLIN G. Duration: no carditis = 5yr/to age 21. Carditis = 10yr/to age 21. Persistent valvular disease = LIFELONG. Prevents recurrent strep -> recurrent ARF.

rows: Monthly IM benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units
5 years or until age 21, whichever is longer

questions.stem: A child with recurrent asthma and family history of atopy. Best long-term stabiliser?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: INHALED CORTICOSTEROID = first-line CONTROLLER (preventive, long-term stabiliser) for persistent asthma. Reduces airway inflammation. Used DAILY regardless of symptoms. Fluticasone, budesonide, beclometasone are examples.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SABA = RELIEVER for ACUTE symptoms only. Using SABA daily (>2x/week) indicates inadequate control and need for ICS controller. NOT a long-term stabiliser.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Montelukast is a LEUKOTRIENE RECEPTOR ANTAGONIST — oral tablet, not inhaled. Used as step-up therapy alongside ICS. NOT first-line controller.

questions.keyPoint: PERSISTENT ASTHMA long-term stabiliser = INHALED CORTICOSTEROID (ICS). ICS prevents. SABA rescues. Never use SABA alone as long-term management in persistent asthma.

rows: Reliever only (salbutamol/albuterol): PRN for acute symptoms

questions.stem: A patient has brownish coloration around the sclera and decreased serum ceruloplasmin. Diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: WILSON DISEASE (hepatolenticular degeneration): autosomal recessive + ATP7B mutation -> copper accumulation in liver + brain + eyes. KAYSER-FLEISCHER RINGS = copper deposits in DESCEMET'S MEMBRANE of cornea (brownish ring around iris/sclera junction). Low ceruloplasmin. Low serum copper but HIGH urine copper. Liver disease + neuropsychiatric symptoms + KF rings = Wilson's. Diagnosis: KF rings on slit-lamp + low ceruloplasmin + high 24hr urine copper. Liver biopsy: high copper. Treatment: D-penicillamine (chelation) or trientine.

questions.keyPoint: WILSON DISEASE: Kayser-Fleischer rings (brownish ring at corneal limbus) + LOW ceruloplasmin + liver disease + neuropsychiatric symptoms. Autosomal recessive. ATP7B mutation.

rows: Copper deposits in Descemet's membrane — slit lamp required
LOW (paradoxically, though ceruloplasmin is copper carrier)

questions.stem: An infant with cow's milk protein allergy. Appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Soy formula was previously used but is NO LONGER recommended as first-line: (1) 15-40% of CMPA infants are ALSO allergic to soy (cross-reactivity). (2) Soy contains phytoestrogens. (3) eHF is preferred.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CMPA management: EXTENSIVELY HYDROLYSED FORMULA (eHF) = first-line for formula-fed infants. Proteins are broken into small peptides -> no allergenic epitopes -> tolerated. If eHF fails -> AMINO ACID FORMULA (elemental). Continue breastfeeding if breastfed (mother avoids dairy). 90% of CMPA resolves by age 3 years.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ORS is for dehydration from gastroenteritis. Not appropriate management for CMPA.

questions.keyPoint: CMPA management: EXTENSIVELY HYDROLYSED FORMULA (eHF) as first-line. NOT soy formula (40% cross-reactivity). If breastfed: continue breastfeeding + mother avoids dairy. Resolves in 90% by age 3.

rows: Mother avoids all dairy while breastfeeding

questions.stem: A girl with coarctation of the aorta and short stature. Underlying syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TURNER SYNDROME (45,X0): SHORT STATURE (most constant feature) + COARCTATION OF AORTA (15-35% + bicuspid aortic valve) + webbed neck + low hairline + wide-carrying angle (cubitus valgus) + primary amenorrhoea + streak gonads + lymphoedema at birth. Treatment: GH therapy + oestrogen replacement for puberty.

questions.keyPoint: TURNER SYNDROME: SHORT STATURE + COARCTATION OF AORTA + webbed neck + primary amenorrhoea + streak gonads. 45,X0. GH therapy + oestrogen replacement. ECHO mandatory for cardiac defects.

rows: Coarctation of aorta + bicuspid aortic valve
Short stature + webbed neck + primary amenorrhoea + lymphoedema

questions.stem: An 8-year-old with periorbital edema, tea-colored urine, hypertension, and elevated ASO titer. Most likely histopathological finding on renal biopsy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IgA NEPHROPATHY — NOT PSGN. Key differentiator.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PSGN histopathology: EM = SUBEPITHELIAL HUMP-LIKE deposits (IgG + C3). LM = diffuse proliferative GN with endocapillary proliferation + neutrophil infiltration. IF = granular IgG + C3 deposits ("starry sky" pattern). These humps are pathognomonic of PSGN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MCD: normal on LM; foot process effacement on EM. No immune deposits.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: RPGN / severe GN. Not typical uncomplicated PSGN.

questions.keyPoint: PSGN BIOPSY: subepithelial hump-like immune complex deposits (EM) + "starry sky" IgG/C3 (IF) + diffuse proliferative GN (LM). Humps are pathognomonic of PSGN.

rows: Granular IgG + C3 starry sky pattern

questions.stem: A child with sensorineural hearing loss and glomerulonephritis progressing to renal failure. What type of collagen mutation is responsible?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Collagen type I = bone, skin, tendons. Defects in COL1A1/A2 = OSTEOGENESIS IMPERFECTA (brittle bones + blue sclerae + dentinogenesis imperfecta). Not kidney or hearing loss.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ALPORT SYNDROME: mutations in COL4A3, COL4A4, or COL4A5 genes (TYPE IV COLLAGEN = component of GLOMERULAR BASEMENT MEMBRANE + cochlear basement membrane + ocular basement membrane). X-linked (COL4A5 most common, males severely affected) or AR (COL4A3/A4). Features: haematuria + progressive GN -> ESRD + sensorineural hearing loss + ocular abnormalities (anterior lenticonus).

questions.keyPoint: ALPORT SYNDROME: COL4A3/A4/A5 mutation (TYPE IV COLLAGEN) -> GBM abnormality -> haematuria + ESRD + sensorineural hearing loss + ocular defects. X-linked (males worst). Thin BM on EM initially -> progressive ESRD.

rows: Haematuria + progressive GN (ESRD) + SNHL + anterior lenticonus

questions.stem: Question about ABO incompatibility in the newborn. Full options not fully recalled by source student.
questions.keyPoint: ABO incompatibility: O+ mother + A or B baby = anti-A or anti-B IgG crosses placenta -> haemolysis -> jaundice in first 24 hours. Positive Coombs. Treatment: phototherapy + exchange transfusion if severe.

rows: O mother has IgG anti-A/anti-B -> crosses placenta -> haemolyses fetal RBCs
Phototherapy; exchange transfusion for rapidly rising or severe bilirubin
None specific — Anti-D is for Rh, not ABO

flag.note: Recalled question — the source marks its options/answer as incomplete. Study the concept and verify the key.

questions.stem: In iron deficiency anaemia, what is seen on the blood film?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (>5 lobes) = MEGALOBLASTIC ANAEMIA (B12 or folate deficiency). Not IDA.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Spherocytes = Hereditary Spherocytosis (DAT negative) or AIHA (DAT positive). Not IDA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pale central area (hypochromic) with pencil cells
choices.explanation: IDA BLOOD FILM: hypochromic (central pallor) + microcytic RBCs + PENCIL (CIGAR) CELLS (elongated RBCs) + target cells. Hypochromia = insufficient Hb in cells. Microcytosis = small RBCs. Pencil cells = specific to IDA.

questions.keyPoint: IDA BLOOD FILM: HYPOCHROMIC + MICROCYTIC RBCs + PENCIL (CIGAR) CELLS. Hypersegmented neutrophils = megaloblastic. Spherocytes = HS or AIHA. Bite cells = G6PD. Target cells = thalassaemia.

rows: Hypochromic microcytic + pencil/cigar cells + target cells

questions.stem: A child with minimal change nephrotic syndrome. Most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MCD first-line treatment = ORAL PREDNISOLONE 2mg/kg/day (max 60mg) for 4-6 weeks then taper. >90% response rate (urine protein clears). STANDARD PROTOCOL for childhood nephrotic syndrome without biopsy (in typical presentation age 1-8).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ACE inhibitors reduce proteinuria (antiproteinuric effect) but do NOT TREAT MCD. Used as adjunct in refractory cases or when nephrotic syndrome causes hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cyclophosphamide is SECOND-LINE for FREQUENT RELAPSERS or STEROID-DEPENDENT/RESISTANT MCD. NOT first-line for initial episode.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Fluid restriction is used cautiously in severe oedema with hyponatraemia. NOT a specific treatment for MCD.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Albumin infusions are used for symptomatic management of SEVERE hypoalbuminaemia + oedema. Temporary effect only. NOT a specific treatment for MCD.

questions.keyPoint: MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE = PREDNISOLONE 2mg/kg/day first-line. >90% respond. Treat empirically without biopsy in typical childhood nephrotic syndrome (age 1-8, no haematuria, normal BP, normal C3).

questions.stem: A neonate's abdominal X-ray shows pneumatosis intestinalis. What is a major risk factor for this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Phototherapy is used for neonatal jaundice. Not a cause of NEC. If anything, indirect hyperbilirubinaemia may slightly increase gut permeability but phototherapy itself is not a direct NEC risk factor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Infection contributes to NEC (bacterial overgrowth from E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter) but is not the PRIMARY identified risk factor listed here. Formula feeding is the key modifiable risk factor.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: NEC risk factors include: PREMATURITY (biggest risk) + formula feeding (breastfeeding reduces NEC risk 6-10x) + perinatal hypoxia/asphyxia + abnormal gut colonisation. PNEUMATOSIS INTESTINALIS (gas in bowel wall from bacterial invasion) = PATHOGNOMONIC of NEC.

questions.keyPoint: NEC RISK FACTORS: Prematurity (biggest) + FORMULA FEEDING (breastfeeding is protective) + perinatal asphyxia + gut ischaemia. PNEUMATOSIS INTESTINALIS = pathognomonic of NEC.

rows: Pneumatosis intestinalis (gas in bowel wall) on X-ray
NPO + NG decompression + IV antibiotics + surgical resection

questions.stem: A child develops thrombocytopenia after a viral infection. What is the pathophysiological mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ITP MECHANISM: viral infection -> MOLECULAR MIMICRY or non-specific immune activation -> IgG AUTOANTIBODIES against platelet surface antigens (GPIIb/IIIa) -> antibody-coated platelets destroyed by macrophages in SPLEEN -> thrombocytopenia. This is the classic immune mechanism.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bone marrow suppression (aplastic anaemia, parvovirus B19) causes PANCYTOPENIA (all cell lines). ITP causes ISOLATED THROMBOCYTOPENIA — bone marrow megakaryocytes are actually INCREASED (compensating).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Platelet consumption in microthrombi = TTP or HUS (MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI). ITP is IMMUNE DESTRUCTION, not consumption. No MAHA in ITP.

questions.keyPoint: ITP MECHANISM = IgG AUTOANTIBODIES against platelet GPIIb/IIIa -> spleen destroys antibody-coated platelets. Bone marrow megakaryocytes are INCREASED (compensating). ISOLATED thrombocytopenia = Hb and WBC normal.

rows: IgG autoantibodies -> platelet destruction in spleen (Fc receptor-mediated)
Aplastic = ALL cell lines reduced; ITP = ONLY platelets
TTP = platelet CONSUMPTION in microthrombi + MAHA + neurological
IVIG (blocks Fc receptors on spleen macrophages) or prednisolone
